options: Pixels as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.
Triangles using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Points within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: 3D pixels — volumetric elements. Store density, material, color per cell. Used for terrain, medical imaging, UE Voxel Plugin.

questions.e: Each node: 0 or 8 children. Subdivide when cell has too many objects. UE lightmass, some collision queries, voxel plugins all use octrees.
questions.explanation: SVO only allocates nodes for regions containing data. Vast empty spaces have zero nodes. Memory usage scales with surface area (~O(n²)) rather than volume (~O(n³)). Enables high-resolution voxel data (e.g., 4096³) that would be impossible with dense grids.
questions.question: What is an octree used for?
options: Only rendering through the built-in API
Spatial indexing: 3D recursive subdivision into 8 octants. Used for collision, LOD, voxel compression, light mapping
Only collision as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only storage using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Spatial indexing: 3D recursive subdivision into 8 octants. Used for collision, LOD, voxel compression, light mapping

questions.e: SVO: only allocate where geometry exists. Vast empty spaces = no nodes. Enables high-resolution voxel data without memory explosion.
questions.explanation: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) steps ray through voxel grid: compute t to next X, Y, and Z boundaries; advance to the closest one. Each step is O(1). Total cost = O(number of voxels traversed). Used in voxel ray tracing and Minecraft-style rendering.
questions.question: How does sparse voxel octree (SVO) save memory?
options: Compression in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only store nodes that contain data — skip empty regions. Dense grid = O(n³); SVO = O(surface area).
It doesn't via the core framework
Texture storage in most implementations with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Only store nodes that contain data — skip empty regions. Dense grid = O(n³); SVO = O(surface area).

questions.e: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer): advance ray to next voxel boundary. O(number of voxels traversed). Used in voxel ray tracing, Minecraft-style games.
questions.explanation: A voxel represents a value on a regular 3D grid — the 3D analog of a pixel. Each voxel stores data (density, material ID, color). Marching Cubes converts voxel data to polygon meshes. Used in medical imaging, terrain generation, UE Voxel Plugin.
questions.question: What is ray-voxel traversal?
options: A rendering method in most implementations
Marching ray through voxel grid: step to next voxel boundary along each axis. Efficient for ray casting in voxel worlds.
A collision method across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A navigation method within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Marching ray through voxel grid: step to next voxel boundary along each axis. Efficient for ray casting in voxel worlds.

questions.question: What is FName?
options: A regular string for typical use cases across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
A wide string across all supported platforms with additional processing within the stan
Deprecated for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Interned, immutable string — stored in global table, O(1) comparison via index
questions.explanation: FName: hash table index. Comparing two FNames = comparing integers. Used for asset paths, identifiers. Case-insensitive.

questions.e: FText = user-facing, localizable. FString = mutable, gameplay use. FName = interned, identifiers. Three distinct use cases.
questions.explanation: FName is an index into the global TNameEntryArray table. operator== compares two integers (table indices), not strings. This O(1) comparison makes FNames ideal for asset paths, identifiers, and gameplay tags where comparison speed matters.
questions.question: What is FText?
options: Same as FString for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.
A hash as defined in the specification with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxx
A path by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Localizable display text — supports translation, pluralization. Used for UI. Not for gameplay logic.

questions.e: TCHAR abstracts platform encoding. TEXT("hello") = L"hello" on Windows, "hello" elsewhere. FString is TArray<TCHAR>.
questions.explanation: FText wraps a display string with localization support: translation tables, pluralization rules, culture-specific formatting. Used for UI text that players see. FString is for gameplay logic; FName is for identifiers. Never mix them.
questions.question: What is TCHAR?
options: A string class across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.
A string type for typical use cases
A macro for typical use cases with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UE's character type — wchar_t on Windows (UTF-16), char on others. TEXT() macro ensures correct encoding.

questions.e: FName = (index, number). Index into global TNameEntryArray. == operator compares indices. String comparison is never done at runtime.
questions.explanation: The FName pool is a fixed-size hash table created at engine startup. All FNames exist forever — you cannot remove them. Creating FNames from runtime-generated strings (player names, chat messages) would eventually overflow the pool. Use FString for dynamic text.
questions.question: Why is FName comparison O(1)?
options: It compares strings across all supported platforms in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)
It uses hash in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's sorted in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: FName stores an index into a global name table — comparing two FNames is comparing two integers (table indices)

questions.e: FNames live forever in the global pool. Don't create FNames from dynamic strings (player names, chat messages). Use FString for runtime-generated text.
questions.explanation: TCHAR abstracts platform character encoding. On Windows: wchar_t (UTF-16). On other platforms: char (UTF-8). The TEXT() macro ensures string literals have the correct encoding. FString = TArray<TCHAR> internally.
questions.question: What is the FName pool and why does it matter?
options: Memory leak via the core framework
A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).
Garbage collection for typical use cases with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Thread pool using standard engine approaches with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A fixed-size hash table of all FNames ever created. Can't be cleared at runtime. Too many unique FNames = overflow (assert).

questions.question: Why does UE use MoveTemp instead of std::move?
options: Same thing through the built-in API with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
Different behavior under normal conditions with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: UE wrapper — same semantics but consistent naming convention. MoveTemp(x) ≡ std::move(x).
questions.explanation: UE naming convention: MoveTemp for rvalue cast. MoveTempIfPossible for conditional move. All wrap standard move semantics.

questions.e: MoveTemp = UE naming for std::move. MoveTempIfPossible = conditional move. Same underlying mechanics as standard move semantics.
questions.explanation: MoveTempIfPossible is UE's conditional move: if T has a move constructor, cast to rvalue (enable move); otherwise leave as lvalue (copy). Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific. Used in TArray growth and container operations.
questions.question: What does MoveTemp do?
options: Copies by default behavior
UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.
Deletes as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Allocates as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UE's std::move — casts to rvalue reference. MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<T&&>(x). Enables move semantics.

questions.e: MoveTempIfPossible checks TIsMoveConstructible<T>. Falls back to copy for non-movable types. Used in TArray growth path.
questions.explanation: UE disables C++ exceptions globally. Without exceptions, there's no exception-safety concern during move. TArray can always move elements on reallocation without worrying about a partial move leaving the array corrupted if an exception is thrown.
questions.question: What does MoveTempIfPossible do?
options: Always moves for typical use cases
Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.
Always copies across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deletes for typical use cases with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Conditionally moves if T is movable, otherwise copies. Similar to std::move_if_noexcept but UE-specific.

questions.e: No exceptions = no exception-safety concern. TArray doesn't need move_if_noexcept. Always moves elements on reallocation.
questions.explanation: FORCEINLINE expands to __forceinline (MSVC) or __attribute__((always_inline)) (Clang/GCC). MoveTemp is a tiny template that MUST be zero-cost — forcing inlining guarantees there's no function call overhead for what should just be a cast.
questions.question: Why is TArray always able to move on reallocation?
options: It copies within the current scope
UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.
It doesn't move as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Only for POD as defined in the specification with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UE disables exceptions globally — every type is effectively noexcept-move. TArray always moves, never falls back to copy.

questions.e: MoveTemp is a template that MUST be inlined. FORCEINLINE = __forceinline on MSVC, always_inline on Clang. Zero function call overhead.
questions.explanation: MoveTemp(x) ≡ static_cast<typename TRemoveReference<T>::Type&&>(x). It's a cast — it doesn't move anything. It enables the move constructor or move assignment operator to be selected by overload resolution. Same mechanism as std::move.
questions.question: What is FORCEINLINE and why is it used on MoveTemp?
options: Debug only as defined in the specification
Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.
Style as defined in the specification with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Forces inlining even in debug builds — MoveTemp must be zero-cost. Essential for performance-critical paths.

questions.question: What is TObjectPtr?
options: A raw pointer by default behavior
GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
A shared pointer through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated via the core framework with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: GC-tracked smart pointer for UObjects — transparent replacement for raw T* with lazy loading and editor integration
questions.explanation: TObjectPtr<T> replaces raw T* in UE5. Implicit conversion to T*. GC tracking. Lazy object resolution. Editor cook integration.

questions.e: TSharedPtr: non-UObject ref-counting. TSharedRef: non-nullable variant. TWeakPtr: non-owning observer. For UObjects use TObjectPtr.
questions.explanation: TSharedPtr is reference-counted (like std::shared_ptr) for non-UObject types (FVector, custom structs). UObjects use UE's garbage collector — adding shared_ptr refcounting would conflict with GC. Use TSharedPtr for plain C++ objects.
questions.question: What is TSharedPtr?
options: Same as TObjectPtr via the core framework with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr
For UObjects in most implementations with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Reference-counted smart pointer for NON-UObject types — similar to std::shared_ptr

questions.e: TUniquePtr<T> = exclusive heap ownership. Cannot be copied. Move transfers ownership. Automatically deletes on destruction.
questions.explanation: TUniquePtr provides exclusive heap ownership for non-UObject types. Cannot be copied (deleted copy constructor). Moving transfers ownership. Automatically calls delete on destruction. UE's equivalent of std::unique_ptr.
questions.question: What is TUniquePtr?
options: Same as TSharedPtr as defined in the specification with additional processing with the def
Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.
For UObjects for typical use cases
Deprecated under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Exclusive ownership — UE's std::unique_ptr. Non-copyable, movable. For non-UObject types.

questions.e: TWeakObjectPtr = observer. Doesn't keep UObject alive. Used for optional references, back-pointers that shouldn't prevent GC.
questions.explanation: TWeakObjectPtr observes a UObject without preventing GC. Must call IsValid() before dereferencing — the object may have been garbage collected. Used for optional references, back-pointers, and cached lookups that shouldn't extend lifetime.
questions.question: What is TWeakObjectPtr?
options: Same as TObjectPtr through the built-in API with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.
Strong reference within the current scope
Deprecated as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Non-owning reference to UObject — doesn't prevent GC. Must check IsValid() before use.

questions.e: UObject lifetime is managed by UE's garbage collector. Adding shared_ptr refcounting would create two competing ownership systems.
questions.explanation: UObjects have their own mark-and-sweep garbage collector. std::shared_ptr tracks reference counts independently — the two systems would compete and potentially double-delete or leak. UE provides TObjectPtr for GC-tracked ownership instead.
questions.question: Why can't you use std::shared_ptr for UObjects?
options: It's slower using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.
It doesn't compile via the core framework in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Memory usage in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: UObjects have their own GC — std::shared_ptr's reference counting would conflict. UE GC manages UObject lifetime.

questions.question: What is TArray's growth strategy?
options: Fixed size via the core framework
Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
Never grows using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Cubic growth in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Similar to std::vector — exponential growth (UE adds slack on reallocation). TArray::Add() reallocates when full.
questions.explanation: TArray = dynamic array. Similar to vector. Grow by adding slack (default 4 elements at small sizes). Reserve() prevents reallocation.

questions.e: Emplace like emplace_back. Avoids temporary creation. TArray::Emplace(Args...) forwards to element constructor at new end position.
questions.explanation: Reserve(N) pre-allocates capacity to at least N elements. Subsequent Add() calls don't reallocate until size exceeds N. Use when you know the final size upfront — avoids all reallocation overhead during population.
questions.question: What does TArray::Emplace do vs Add?
options: Same via the core framework
Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.
Add is faster for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Emplace is deprecated using standard engine approaches with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Emplace constructs in-place at end — forwards args to constructor. Add copies/moves existing object. Emplace avoids temporary.

questions.e: Reserve(N) = allocate N slots upfront. Push N elements without reallocation. Same as std::vector::reserve.
questions.explanation: UE's TArray adds Slack (default=4) plus max(old_capacity, Slack) on each growth. Small arrays grow by a few elements; large arrays grow proportionally. This amortized O(1) growth is similar to std::vector's exponential strategy.
questions.question: What does TArray::Reserve do?
options: Shrinks array within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds
Sorts through the built-in API
Clears under normal conditions with additional processing with regular usage patterns
questions.correctAnswer: Pre-allocates capacity to at least N — prevents reallocation during subsequent Adds

questions.e: UE adds Slack (default=4) each growth. Also grows by old_capacity. Larger arrays grow faster. Amortized O(1) Add like std::vector.
questions.explanation: TArray::SetNum(N) resizes to exactly N elements. If N > current size: default-constructs new elements (or uses specified value). If N < current size: destroys excess elements. Does NOT change capacity — only changes the logical size.
questions.question: What is TArray's growth strategy when full?
options: Fixed size across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.
Doubles always across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
No growth by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Adds slack: new_capacity = old_capacity + max(old_capacity, Slack). Exponential growth for amortized O(1) Add.

questions.e: SetNum(N) = resize to N. If N > size: default-construct new elements. If N < size: destroy excess elements. Does NOT change capacity.
questions.explanation: Emplace forwards constructor arguments directly to the new element's constructor at the end of the array — no temporary object, no copy, no move. Same as std::vector::emplace_back. Use Emplace(args...) when constructing complex objects in-place.
questions.question: What does TArray::SetNum do?
options: Same as Add via the core framework with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess
Reserves across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Sorts by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Resizes array to N elements — initializes new elements (default or specified value), destroys excess

questions.question: What data structure does TMap use?
options: Red-black tree under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
Sorted array via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
B-tree by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Hash table with separate chaining — O(1) average, similar to std::unordered_map
questions.explanation: TMap = unordered hash map. Uses sparse array with chaining. Key must have GetTypeHash(). No ordering guarantees. O(1) average lookup.

questions.e: FVector* val = Map.Find(key); if(val) use(*val); Standard UE pattern — pointer-based lookup, no exceptions.
questions.explanation: TMap uses GetTypeHash(var T&) to compute the hash bucket. Built-in types (int, float, FString, FName) have predefined GetTypeHash. Custom structs must provide a specialization: friend uint32 GetTypeHash(var FMyStruct& S).
questions.question: What does TMap::Find do?
options: Throws if not found via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.
Returns default using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxx
Always succeeds under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Returns pointer to value if key exists, nullptr otherwise. No exception.

questions.e: TMap requires GetTypeHash(var T&). Built-in types, FString, FName have it. Custom structs: add friend uint32 GetTypeHash(var FMyStruct&).
questions.explanation: FindOrAdd returns a reference to the value for the given key. If the key doesn't exist, it inserts a default-constructed value and returns a reference to it. Useful pattern: TMap[key].Count++ or auto& Val = Map.FindOrAdd(Key); Val = ComputeValue().
questions.question: What hash function does TMap require?
options: std::hash within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.
Always works as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
CRC32 via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: GetTypeHash() — must be defined for key type. UE provides for built-in types. Custom types need specialization.

questions.e: FindOrAdd: returns existing value or creates new entry. Useful for counters, caches. TMap[Key] = TMap::FindOrAdd(Key) + 1 style patterns.
questions.explanation: TMap enforces unique keys — inserting a duplicate key overwrites the existing value. TMultiMap allows multiple entries with the same key — Find() returns the first match; use TMultiMap::MultiFind() to get all values for a key.
questions.question: What does TMap::FindOrAdd do?
options: Always adds in most implementations
Returns reference to value for key, creating default-constructed value if key doesn't exist
Only finds in most implementations with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxx
Throws across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Returns reference to value for key, creating default-constructed value if key doesn't exist

questions.e: TMap: unique keys. TMultiMap: multiple entries with same key. TMultiMap::Find returns first match. Use for key→many relationships.
questions.explanation: TMap::Find(Key) returns a pointer to the value if found, nullptr if not. No exceptions. Pattern: if (FVector* Val = Map.Find(Key)) { Use(*Val); }. This is the preferred lookup pattern in UE — explicit null check, no exception overhead.
questions.question: How is TMap different from TMultiMap?
options: Same through the built-in API
TMap: one value per key (unique). TMultiMap: multiple values per key (duplicate keys allowed).
TMap is faster via the core framework with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
TMultiMap is deprecated via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: TMap: one value per key (unique). TMultiMap: multiple values per key (duplicate keys allowed).

questions.question: What does UHT (Unreal Header Tool) do?
options: Compiles code as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Parses UCLASS/USTRUCT/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection data — .generated.h files with StaticClass(), serialization, GC info
Nothing using standard engine approaches with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Optimizes for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Parses UCLASS/USTRUCT/UFUNCTION macros and generates reflection data — .generated.h files with StaticClass(), serialization, GC info
questions.explanation: UHT pre-processes headers before the compiler. UCLASS→StaticClass(), class flags. UPROPERTY→serialization, replication. UFUNCTION→network RPC, Blueprint.

questions.e: UPROPERTY = reflection + GC. Without it: no GC tracking (dangling!), no serialization (can't save/load), no Blueprint/network access.
questions.explanation: UPROPERTY() enables: GC tracking (prevents garbage collection of referenced objects), serialization (save/load), replication (network sync), Blueprint access (read/write in Blueprint graphs), and property window editing. Without it: none of these work.
questions.question: What does UPROPERTY() do?
options: Nothing for typical use cases
Marks member for GC tracking, serialization, replication, Blueprint access — UHT generates reflection code for marked properties
Only a comment under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated for typical use cases for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Marks member for GC tracking, serialization, replication, Blueprint access — UHT generates reflection code for marked properties

questions.e: UFUNCTION enables BlueprintCallable, Server/Client/NetMulticast RPCs, Exec (console). UHT generates thunks that marshal parameters.
questions.explanation: UFUNCTION() enables: BlueprintCallable (called from Blueprint graphs), Server/Client/NetMulticast (network RPCs), Exec (console commands). UHT generates thunk functions that marshal parameters between C++ and the Blueprint virtual machine.
questions.question: What does UFUNCTION() do?
options: Nothing within the current scope
Marks function for Blueprint call, network RPC, or exec command — UHT generates thunk code for cross-boundary calls
Only a comment using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated for typical use cases with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Marks function for Blueprint call, network RPC, or exec command — UHT generates thunk code for cross-boundary calls

questions.e: BlueprintCallable = void/impure function (white exec pins). BlueprintPure = var function (green, no pins). Use Pure for getters, math.
questions.explanation: BlueprintCallable functions have execution pins (white) and can modify state — they run when called. BlueprintPure functions have no exec pins (green) and cannot modify state — they're evaluated on demand like a getter. Use Pure for var accessors and math.
questions.question: What is the difference between BlueprintCallable and BlueprintPure?
options: Same as defined in the specification
Callable: has execution pin (can modify state). Pure: no exec pin (can't modify), evaluated on demand like a getter.
Pure is faster using standard engine approaches across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Callable is deprecated through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Callable: has execution pin (can modify state). Pure: no exec pin (can't modify), evaluated on demand like a getter.
Server, Client, NetMulticast, Reliable, Unreliable, WithValidation
questions.explanation: Server: client→server. Client: server→owning client. NetMulticast: server→all. Reliable: guaranteed delivery. WithValidation: adds _Validate check.
questions.question: What are the UFUNCTION specifiers for networking?
What is UObject?
options: A regular class via the core framework under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Base class for all UE objects — provides GC, serialization, reflection, networking. Everything inherits from UObject.
A component in most implementations
A macro across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Base class for all UE objects — provides GC, serialization, reflection, networking. Everything inherits from UObject.
questions.explanation: UObject = UE's base. AActor, UActorComponent, UAsset all derive from UObject. GC tracking via GUObjectArray. UPROPERTY for GC references.

questions.e: UCLASS = UE's reflection. StaticClass() returns UClass*. Cast<T> uses class flags. CDO = template instance with default property values.
questions.explanation: The CDO (Class Default Object) is a template instance created once per UClass at engine initialization. NewObject<T>() copies the CDO's property values as starting defaults. Changing CDO properties in the editor changes defaults for all new instances.
questions.question: What does UCLASS() macro generate?
options: Nothing for typical use cases with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
UHT generates: StaticClass() function, class flags (EClassCastFlags), CDO (Class Default Object), reflection data
Only a comment through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A warning for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: UHT generates: StaticClass() function, class flags (EClassCastFlags), CDO (Class Default Object), reflection data

questions.e: CDO = prototype object. Created once per class at engine init. NewObject<T>() copies CDO's properties. Editor defaults = CDO properties.
questions.explanation: NewObject<T>(): allocates memory → copies CDO properties → calls constructor → registers with GC → calls PostInitProperties(). Never use raw 'new' for UObjects — NewObject handles GC registration, property initialization, and object flags.
questions.question: What is the Class Default Object (CDO)?
options: A template as defined in the specification in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The default instance of a UClass — created at class registration. Used as template: new objects copy CDO's property values.
The first instance via the core framework
A garbage collector by default behavior with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: The default instance of a UClass — created at class registration. Used as template: new objects copy CDO's property values.

questions.e: NewObject = UE's object factory. Never use 'new' for UObjects. NewObject handles GC registration, CDO copy, property initialization.
questions.explanation: UE GC uses mark-and-sweep: start from root objects (UGameInstance, UWorld), traverse UPROPERTY references marking reachable objects. Unmarked objects are unreachable → deleted. Raw C++ pointers DON'T count as references — must be UPROPERTY() to prevent GC.
questions.question: What is the UObject creation path?
options: malloc within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
NewObject<T>(): allocates memory, copies CDO properties, calls constructor, registers with GC, calls PostInitProperties
new under normal conditions
CreateDefaultSubobject in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: NewObject<T>(): allocates memory, copies CDO properties, calls constructor, registers with GC, calls PostInitProperties

questions.e: UE GC: mark phase (traverse UPROPERTY graph from roots) → sweep phase (delete unmarked). Raw pointers DON'T prevent GC — must be UPROPERTY.
questions.explanation: UCLASS() is parsed by UHT before compilation. It generates: UClass* StaticClass() function, EClassCastFlags enum, CDO instance, reflection property chain (for serialization/property access), and Blueprint type information.
questions.question: How does UE garbage collection know an object is reachable?
options: Reference counting by default behavior
Mark-and-sweep: starts from root set (UGameInstance, UWorld), follows UPROPERTY references. Unmarked = unreachable = delete.
Manual tracking across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Smart pointers as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Mark-and-sweep: starts from root set (UGameInstance, UWorld), follows UPROPERTY references. Unmarked = unreachable = delete.

questions.question: What are the three network roles in UE?
options: Client only by default behavior
ROLE_Authority (server), ROLE_AutonomousProxy (owning client), ROLE_SimulatedProxy (other clients)
Server only using standard engine approaches with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxx
Peer to peer by default behavior with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: ROLE_Authority (server), ROLE_AutonomousProxy (owning client), ROLE_SimulatedProxy (other clients)
questions.explanation: Authority: server, has final say. Autonomous: client that owns the pawn. Simulated: everyone else's pawn. Role determines execution path.

questions.question: What does HasAuthority() check?
options: Is client by default behavior
Is this machine the network authority (server) for this actor?
Is dedicated server within the current scope in standard configuratio
Is listen server using standard engine approaches in most practical sc
questions.correctAnswer: Is this machine the network authority (server) for this actor?
questions.explanation: HasAuthority() = GetLocalRole() == ROLE_Authority. Use this to guard server-only logic: spawning, damage, state changes.

questions.e: AutonomousProxy = 'this is my pawn.' Input prediction runs here. Client RPCs delivered here. Server sees this actor as ROLE_Authority.
questions.explanation: ROLE_AutonomousProxy is the role of a pawn on the client that owns it. This client processes input prediction, receives Client RPCs, and has authoritative-appearing control. The server sees this same pawn as ROLE_Authority.
questions.question: What does ROLE_AutonomousProxy mean?
options: Server for typical use cases with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The client that OWNS this actor — receives client RPCs, runs input prediction. Only one client has this role per actor.
Other clients using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
All clients in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: The client that OWNS this actor — receives client RPCs, runs input prediction. Only one client has this role per actor.

questions.e: Dedicated server: all actors have local role = ROLE_Authority. No autonomous proxies (no local player). No simulated proxies (no rendering).
questions.explanation: On a dedicated server, every actor has GetLocalRole() == ROLE_Authority — the server is authoritative for everything. There are no AutonomousProxy roles (no local players) and no SimulatedProxy roles (no rendering clients).
questions.question: What does GetLocalRole() return on a dedicated server?
options: ROLE_SimulatedProxy as defined in the specification
ROLE_Authority — server is always the authority for all actors
ROLE_AutonomousProxy within the current scope with additional processing
ROLE_None through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: ROLE_Authority — server is always the authority for all actors

questions.e: SimulatedProxy = 'someone else's pawn.' Receives replicated position, velocity. No input processing. Smoothly interpolates server state.
questions.explanation: ROLE_SimulatedProxy is the role of pawns owned by OTHER players on your client. You receive replicated position/velocity and smoothly interpolate. No input processing — you can't control other players' characters. No Client RPC delivery to you.
questions.question: What ROLE does a pawn have on other clients?
options: Authority through the built-in API
ROLE_SimulatedProxy — other clients simulate the pawn based on replicated state, no input
AutonomousProxy across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
None using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: ROLE_SimulatedProxy — other clients simulate the pawn based on replicated state, no input

questions.question: What does UFUNCTION(Server) do?
options: Nothing through the built-in API with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Marks a function as Server RPC — called on client, executes on server. UFUNCTION(Client) = opposite. UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) = all.
Only in Blueprints by default behavior
Deprecated using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Marks a function as Server RPC — called on client, executes on server. UFUNCTION(Client) = opposite. UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) = all.
questions.explanation: Server RPC: client→server. Client RPC: server→owning client. NetMulticast: server→all clients. Must have _Implementation suffix on definition.

questions.e: Server RPC: declared in header with UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable). Implementation: MyFunc_Implementation(). Client calls MyFunc() → server executes _Implementation.
questions.explanation: Server RPC: client calls MyFunc(), UE serializes parameters, sends to server, server calls MyFunc_Implementation(). Must be called from a client-owned actor. Server validates and executes. Used for: 'I want to jump', 'I fired my weapon'.
questions.question: What does UFUNCTION(Server, Reliable) do?
options: Only server calls via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Client calls → executes on server. Reliable = guaranteed delivery in order. Must have _Implementation suffix.
Only client calls in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Multicast under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Client calls → executes on server. Reliable = guaranteed delivery in order. Must have _Implementation suffix.

questions.e: NetMulticast: server invokes, all clients execute. No _Implementation suffix (called directly). Use for explosions, announcements, global effects.
questions.explanation: NetMulticast: server calls → executes on server AND all connected clients simultaneously. No _Implementation suffix needed. Used for effects everyone must see: explosions, environmental changes, global announcements. Called only from server.
questions.question: What does UFUNCTION(NetMulticast) do?
options: Client to server in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Server calls → executes on server AND all clients. Used for effects everyone should see.
Server only by default behavior
Client only in most implementations with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Server calls → executes on server AND all clients. Used for effects everyone should see.

questions.e: Reliable = TCP-like. Unreliable = UDP-like. Use Reliable for critical events (damage, death). Use Unreliable for cosmetic (footsteps, muzzle flash).
questions.explanation: Reliable RPCs are guaranteed delivery in order — backed by reliable UDP buffer. They can cause head-of-line blocking if the buffer fills. Unreliable RPCs may be dropped and have no ordering guarantees. Use Reliable for critical state; Unreliable for cosmetic effects.
questions.question: What is the difference between Reliable and Unreliable RPCs?
options: Same across all supported platforms
Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered, can bottleneck. Unreliable: may drop, no guarantee, faster. Use Unreliable for frequent/fire-and-forget.
Unreliable is faster via the core framework in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reliable is deprecated under normal conditions with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reliable: guaranteed delivery, ordered, can bottleneck. Unreliable: may drop, no guarantee, faster. Use Unreliable for frequent/fire-and-forget.

questions.e: UFUNCTION(Server, WithValidation). Server calls MyFunc_Validate(params) → if false, kicks client. Validates damage values, ability usage, movement limits.
questions.explanation: WithValidation adds a _Validate function. Before the server executes the RPC, it calls _Validate(params). If _Validate returns false, the RPC is blocked and the client may be disconnected. Used as anti-cheat: validate damage amounts, ability cooldowns, movement speeds.
questions.question: What does WithValidation add to an RPC?
options: Nothing in most implementations
Adds _Validate function — server checks parameters before executing. Returns false to block. Anti-cheat measure.
Speed across all supported platforms within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Multicast via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Adds _Validate function — server checks parameters before executing. Returns false to block. Anti-cheat measure.

questions.question: What does UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Health) do?
options: Nothing as defined in the specification
When Health replicates from server to client, OnRep_Health() callback is called on client — add effects, UI updates
Prevents replication using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Makes it var using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When Health replicates from server to client, OnRep_Health() callback is called on client — add effects, UI updates
questions.explanation: ReplicatedUsing enables callback on value change. Used for reactive effects: damage flash, health bar update. OnRep receives the OLD value for comparison.

questions.e: ReplicatedUsing: client receives new value → OnRep callback fires. Used for reactive UI, damage effects, state transitions.
questions.explanation: Replicated: variable updates silently on clients. ReplicatedUsing: variable updates AND OnRep callback fires. Use Replicated for passive state (position); use ReplicatedUsing when changes need visual/audio response (health bar flash, death animation).
questions.question: What does UPROPERTY(ReplicatedUsing=OnRep_Func) trigger?
options: Nothing by default behavior
When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI
Server call across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Multicast in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: When property replicates from server to client, OnRep_Func() is called on client — add effects, update UI

questions.e: Use Replicated for position/velocity (handled by movement component). Use ReplicatedUsing for health (need damage flash), state (need animation trigger).
questions.explanation: OnRep fires only on clients when a replicated value arrives from the server. The server must manually trigger effects when it changes the variable. This is a common bug: server changes health → expects damage flash, but OnRep doesn't fire on the server.
questions.question: What is the difference between Replicated and ReplicatedUsing?
options: Same as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.
One is faster as defined in the specification with additional processing with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deprecated by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Replicated: silent update (client variable changes, no callback). ReplicatedUsing: update + callback function invoked.

questions.e: OnRep is client-only. Server: change variable → call effect function manually OR rely on the client to trigger effects via OnRep.
questions.explanation: PushModel is an opt-in UE5 optimization: you explicitly call MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME(Class, Property) when a replicated property changes. Without PushModel, UE compares every replicated property every tick to detect changes — expensive for many properties.
questions.question: Does the server call OnRep when it changes a ReplicatedUsing variable?
options: Yes, always as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.
Sometimes through the built-in API
Only in Blueprints using standard engine approaches with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: No — OnRep only fires on CLIENTS when the replicated value arrives. Server must manually call effects.

questions.e: PushModel: opt-in. You explicitly mark properties dirty. Without it, UE compares ALL replicated properties every tick (expensive). PushModel reduces comparison cost.
questions.explanation: OnRep_Health typically receives the old value for comparison. void OnRep_Health(float OldHealth) — you can compute damage taken = OldHealth - Health. This is more useful than just knowing 'health changed' — you can scale effects by damage magnitude.
questions.question: What is PushModel (UE5) for replication?
options: A rendering technique in most implementations with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxx
Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.
A network protocol within the current scope
Deprecated using standard engine approaches in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Explicit dirty marking: MARK_PROPERTY_DIRTY_FROM_NAME. Only replicates changed properties. Saves bandwidth.

questions.question: What is O(1) and when is it achievable?
options: Any algorithm through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
Only in math within the current scope
Never using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Constant time — independent of input size. Array access, hash table lookup (average). The ideal complexity.
questions.explanation: O(1) = runtime doesn't grow with input. arr[i], map.find(key) average. The holy grail. Rare for complex operations.

questions.question: What is O(n²) and why is it dangerous?
options: Fast using standard engine approaches
Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
Always fine as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
O(1) using standard engine approaches across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Quadratic — nested loops over input. 1000 elements = 1M operations. 100K elements = 10B. Unusable at scale.
questions.explanation: O(n²): bubble sort, naive collision detection. Double the input = 4x the time. Must be avoided at scale. Use hashing or spatial partitioning.

questions.e: log₂(1M) ≈ 20. Binary search, BST find/insert, heap push/pop. Any 'divide problem size by constant each step' algorithm.
questions.explanation: log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20. Binary search halves the search space each step. Balanced BST operations (find, insert, delete) all descend at most the tree height. Each level discards half the remaining possibilities.
questions.question: What is O(log n) and what algorithms achieve it?
options: Constant as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.
Linear across all supported platforms
Quadratic as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Logarithmic — binary search, balanced tree operations. Doubling input = 1 more step. Scales excellently.

questions.e: Ω(n log n) = lower bound for comparison-based sorting. Merge sort, heap sort, quicksort (average) achieve this. Counting sort beats it by NOT comparing.
questions.explanation: Ω(n log n) is a proven lower bound for comparison-based sorting: there are n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit of information, so you need at least log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons to distinguish the correct ordering.
questions.question: What is O(n log n) and why is it the lower bound for comparison sorting?
options: Arbitrary using standard engine approaches
Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.
It could be faster with quantum computing via the core framework with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's not a real bound across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Decision tree argument: n! possible orderings, each comparison gives 1 bit → need log₂(n!) ≈ n log n comparisons. Optimal.

questions.e: Amortized = average over sequence. Push N elements: O(N) total = O(1) amortized per push. Reallocation copies old elements — costs O(old_size).
questions.explanation: Most push_back calls are O(1) (just increment size). Occasionally a push_back triggers O(n) reallocation (allocate new buffer, copy all elements). The occasional expensive operation averages to O(1) per push when amortized over many calls.
questions.question: What is amortized O(1) and how does vector::push_back achieve it?
options: Always O(1) as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.
Never O(1) within the current scope
It doesn't achieve it under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Individual push_back may be O(n) (reallocation), but averaged over N pushes it's O(1) — reallocation happens rarely.

questions.question: What physically stores a single bit in DRAM?
options: A transistor
A capacitor
A magnetic platter
A floating gate
questions.correctAnswer: A capacitor
questions.explanation: DRAM stores each bit as charge in a tiny capacitor. The capacitor leaks charge over time, which is why DRAM needs periodic refreshing (~64ms per row).

questions.question: How many unique values can 8 bits represent?
questions.explanation: 8 bits = 2^8 = 256 unique values. Range depends on interpretation: 0-255 (unsigned) or -128 to 127 (two's complement signed).

questions.question: What is the decimal value of binary 1011?
What does the hexadecimal digit F represent in decimal?
questions.explanation: Hexadecimal uses 0-9 and A-F. A=10, B=11, C=12, D=13, E=14, F=15.

questions.question: How many bytes are in a kilobyte (binary, not decimal)?
questions.explanation: In computing, a kilobyte (KiB) is 2^10 = 1024 bytes. The decimal kilobyte (1000 bytes) is used for storage marketing but not for memory addressing.

questions.question: What is the largest unsigned integer representable in 4 bits?
questions.explanation: 4 bits can represent 2^4 = 16 values (0-15). The largest is 15 (binary 1111).

questions.question: Which numbering system uses digits 0-7?
options: Binary
Octal
Decimal
Hexadecimal
questions.correctAnswer: Octal
questions.explanation: Octal uses base-8 with digits 0-7. It was historically popular because 3 bits = 1 octal digit (e.g., Unix file permissions use octal notation).

questions.question: In two's complement, how do you negate a number?
options: Flip all bits
Flip all bits and add 1
Set the MSB to 1 within th
Subtract from 255 under
questions.correctAnswer: Flip all bits and add 1
questions.explanation: Two's complement negation: invert all bits (one's complement), then add 1. Example: 5 (00000101) → flip (11111010) → add 1 (11111011) = -5.

questions.question: What is the two's complement representation of -1 in 8 bits?
questions.explanation: -1 in two's complement is all bits set to 1. For 8 bits: 11111111. This works because 11111111 + 1 = 00000000 (overflow discarded), giving 0.

questions.question: What is the range of an 8-bit signed integer?
questions.explanation: 8-bit signed: -2^7 to 2^7-1 = -128 to 127. The asymmetry (-128 exists but +128 doesn't) is a two's complement property: 10000000 = -128.

questions.question: In big-endian byte order, where is the most significant byte stored?
options: At the lowest memory address
At the highest memory address
In the CPU register
It depends on the operating system
questions.correctAnswer: At the lowest memory address
questions.explanation: Big-endian stores the most significant byte at the lowest address (the 'big end' first). Network byte order is big-endian. Little-endian (x86) stores the least significant byte first.

questions.question: What problem does sign extension solve?
options: Adding two negative numbers under normal conditions with additional processing
Converting a smaller signed integer to a larger one
Detecting overflow as defined in the specification
Converting between endian formats
questions.correctAnswer: Converting a smaller signed integer to a larger one
questions.explanation: Sign extension copies the sign bit to all new higher bits when widening a signed integer. Example: int8_t -5 (11111011) → int16_t (11111111 11111011) = still -5.

questions.question: What does the x86 instruction `mov eax, [ebx]` do?
options: Copy EAX value into EBX across all supported platforms
Load value at memory address in EBX into EAX
Move the address of EBX into EAX
Compare EAX and EBX as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Load value at memory address in EBX into EAX
questions.explanation: Brackets `[]` denote memory dereference. `mov eax, [ebx]` loads the 32-bit value at the memory address stored in EBX into register EAX.

questions.question: How many general-purpose registers does x86-64 have (excluding stack/base pointer)?
questions.explanation: x86-64 has 16 GPRs: RAX, RBX, RCX, RDX, RSI, RDI, RBP, RSP, and R8-R15. Excluding RSP (stack pointer) and RBP (base pointer), 14 remain for general use.

questions.question: What is the difference between `cmp` and `sub` on x86?
options: cmp modifies the destination, sub doesn't
sub modifies the destination, cmp doesn't
They are identical
cmp works only on registers
questions.correctAnswer: sub modifies the destination, cmp doesn't
questions.explanation: `cmp a, b` computes a-b and sets flags but discards the result. `sub a, b` computes a-b, sets flags, AND stores the result in a. cmp is used before conditional jumps.

questions.question: Which flag is set when an arithmetic operation results in zero?
questions.correctAnswer: ZF
questions.explanation: The Zero Flag (ZF) is set to 1 when an operation produces a zero result. `je` (jump if equal) checks ZF=1. cmp followed by je is how if/else compiles.

questions.question: What does `jne` (jump if not equal) check?
options: ZF=0
CF=1
SF=1
OF=0
questions.correctAnswer: ZF=0
questions.explanation: jne checks if ZF (Zero Flag) is 0, meaning the previous comparison was NOT equal. jne and jnz are the same instruction with different mnemonics.

questions.question: What is a 'nop sled' used for in assembly?
options: Performance optimization
Padding code to align jump targets
Exploit development
All of the above
questions.correctAnswer: All of the above
questions.explanation: NOP (0x90) instructions are multi-purpose: aligning jump targets to cache line boundaries (perf), filling unused space, and in exploit development as a 'landing zone' for shellcode.

questions.question: What are the four stages of the classic CPU instruction cycle?
questions.correctAnswer: Fetch, Decode, Execute, Writeback
questions.explanation: Fetch: load instruction from memory (PC → instruction register). Decode: determine what the instruction does. Execute: ALU performs the operation. Writeback: store result to register.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a branch predictor?
options: To speed up memory access
To guess which way a conditional branch will go
To decode instructions faster
To manage power consumption with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: To guess which way a conditional branch will go
questions.explanation: The branch predictor guesses whether a conditional jump will be taken BEFORE the condition is evaluated. This keeps the pipeline full. A misprediction costs 15-20 cycles as the pipeline is flushed.

questions.question: What does 'superscalar' mean in CPU architecture?
options: The CPU runs at a higher clock speed
The CPU can execute multiple instructions per clock cycle
The CPU has multiple cores within the current scope in standard con
The CPU uses a larger cache via the core framework across c
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU can execute multiple instructions per clock cycle
questions.explanation: Superscalar CPUs have multiple execution units that can process independent instructions simultaneously. Modern CPUs issue 4-8 instructions per cycle (4-wide to 8-wide).

questions.question: What happens during a pipeline stall?
options: The CPU powers down in most implementations
The CPU switches to a different thread across all supported platforms with additional processing
A later pipeline stage waits for an earlier instruction to complete
The clock speed is reduced by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: A later pipeline stage waits for an earlier instruction to complete
questions.explanation: A pipeline stall occurs when an instruction depends on the result of a previous instruction that hasn't completed yet. The dependent instruction must wait, creating a 'bubble' in the pipeline.

questions.question: What is the typical penalty for a branch misprediction on modern CPUs?
questions.explanation: When the branch predictor guesses wrong, the speculatively executed instructions must be discarded and the pipeline restarted from the correct path. This costs ~15-20 cycles on modern x86.

questions.question: What is the typical size of an L1 data cache on a modern CPU core?
options: 8KB
2-4MB
questions.explanation: L1 data cache is typically 32KB (Zen 4) or 48KB (Raptor Lake). It's the smallest and fastest cache, with ~4-5 cycle latency.

questions.question: How many bytes are in an x86 cache line?
questions.explanation: x86 CPUs use 64-byte cache lines. When any byte in a cache line is accessed, the entire 64-byte line is loaded from the next cache level. This is why sequential access is fast (spatial locality).

questions.question: Why does false sharing hurt performance?
options: It increases memory usage in most implementations
Threads on different cores invalidate each other's cache lines
It causes the CPU to use incorrect data with additional processing
It disables the cache entirely across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Threads on different cores invalidate each other's cache lines
questions.explanation: False sharing: two threads modify different variables that happen to share a cache line. Each write invalidates the other core's cache line, causing constant cache misses despite no actual data sharing.

questions.question: What is the approximate latency difference between L1 cache and main memory access?
options: 2x
questions.explanation: L1 cache: ~1ns (4 cycles at 4GHz). DDR5 DRAM: ~80-100ns. That's roughly 100x difference. In CPU cycles: L1 = 4 cycles, DRAM = ~200-400 cycles.

questions.question: What does the MESI protocol ensure?
questions.correctAnswer: Cache coherence
questions.explanation: MESI (Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid) is a cache coherence protocol that ensures all CPU cores see a consistent view of memory. It tracks the state of each cache line across all caches.

questions.question: What does `malloc(size)` return when allocation fails?
options: An invalid pointer
NULL
It throws an exception
questions.correctAnswer: NULL
questions.explanation: malloc returns NULL on failure. Always check the return value. In C++, `new` throws std::bad_alloc (unless `nothrow` is used). UE's `FMemory::Malloc` returns nullptr on failure.

questions.question: What problem does the buddy allocator solve?
options: Memory leaks
External fragmentation
Slow allocation speed
Cache misses across common
questions.correctAnswer: External fragmentation
questions.explanation: The buddy allocator divides memory into power-of-two blocks. When a block is freed, it's merged with its 'buddy' (adjacent equal-sized block), reducing external fragmentation. Used in Linux kernel page allocation.

questions.question: What is internal fragmentation?
questions.correctAnswer: Wasted space inside an allocated block
acceptableAnswers: Wasted space inside an allocated block
questions.explanation: Internal fragmentation: allocated block is larger than requested. Example: requesting 10 bytes, allocator gives 16 bytes (minimum block size). The 6 extra bytes are internal fragmentation.

questions.question: What does `sbrk()` do on Linux?
options: Allocates a specific amount of memory with additional processing
Moves the program break to expand/shrink the heap
Frees allocated memory in most implementations
Locks a memory region via the core framework
questions.correctAnswer: Moves the program break to expand/shrink the heap
questions.explanation: sbrk(increment) adjusts the program's data segment break. Positive increment → expand heap. Negative → shrink. Modern allocators prefer mmap over sbrk for large allocations.

questions.question: Why is `mmap` preferred over `sbrk` for large allocations?
options: mmap is faster across all supported platforms with additional processing
mmap allows returning memory to the OS immediately
mmap uses less memory by default behavior
mmap doesn't require system calls
questions.correctAnswer: mmap allows returning memory to the OS immediately
questions.explanation: sbrk can only grow/shrink at the break boundary. If allocated blocks exist beyond the freed region, sbrk can't shrink. mmap maps independent pages that can be munmap'd immediately regardless of order.

questions.question: What is the size of a standard page on x86-64?
questions.correctAnswer: 4KB
questions.explanation: The standard page size on x86-64 is 4KB (4096 bytes). Huge pages: 2MB (PSE) and 1GB (PDPE). The page size balances TLB coverage vs internal fragmentation.

questions.question: How many levels are in the x86-64 page table hierarchy?
questions.explanation: x86-64 uses 4-level paging: PML4 → PDPT → PD → PT (9 bits per level). Each level has 512 entries. The virtual address is split: [16 unused | 9 PML4 | 9 PDPT | 9 PD | 9 PT | 12 offset].

questions.question: What happens during a TLB miss?
options: The program crashes for typical use cases for common
The CPU performs a page walk to find the translation
The OS swaps the page to disk
The CPU retries the instruction with the default setu
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU performs a page walk to find the translation
questions.explanation: On TLB miss, the MMU walks the page table hierarchy (up to 4 memory accesses for 4-level paging) to find the PTE. The translation is then cached in the TLB for future use.

questions.question: What does the 'dirty' bit in a PTE indicate?
options: The page has been read
The page has been written to
The page is corrupted with additional processing
The page is in swap
questions.correctAnswer: The page has been written to
questions.explanation: The dirty bit indicates the page has been modified since it was loaded. When evicting a page, if dirty=1, the page must be written back to disk. If dirty=0, the page can simply be discarded.

questions.question: What is the purpose of KPTI (Kernel Page Table Isolation)?
questions.correctAnswer: Mitigate the Meltdown vulnerability
questions.explanation: KPTI unmaps kernel pages from userspace page tables to prevent Meltdown attacks. The kernel has its own separate page table. Every syscall must switch page tables, adding 100-500+ cycles overhead.

questions.question: What does `mmap` with MAP_ANONYMOUS do?
options: Maps a file into memory using standard engine approaches
Allocates anonymous memory not backed by a file
Shares memory between processes
Locks memory in RAM across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Allocates anonymous memory not backed by a file
questions.explanation: MAP_ANONYMOUS creates a mapping not backed by any file. It's how malloc typically allocates large blocks: mmap(NULL, size, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE, MAP_PRIVATE|MAP_ANONYMOUS, -1, 0).

questions.question: What does RAID 0 provide?
options: Redundancy in most implementations
Striping for performance
Mirroring across all supported platforms
Error correction
questions.correctAnswer: Striping for performance
questions.explanation: RAID 0 (striping) splits data across multiple disks for parallel read/write, improving throughput. No redundancy — a single disk failure loses all data. Capacity = N × disk_size.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of disks required for RAID 5?
questions.explanation: RAID 5 requires at least 3 disks: data distributed across N-1 disks plus one disk's worth of parity. It can survive one disk failure. Capacity = (N-1) × disk_size.

questions.question: What is the 'write hole' problem in RAID 5?
options: A physical defect in the disk platter in most implementations
Data and parity becoming inconsistent after a crash during a write
A security vulnerability as defined in the specification
A performance bottleneck for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Data and parity becoming inconsistent after a crash during a write
questions.explanation: RAID 5 write requires updating data AND parity on separate disks. If a crash occurs between these two writes, data and parity are inconsistent. ZFS and some hardware RAID controllers use copy-on-write to avoid this.

questions.question: Which RAID level provides mirroring?
questions.correctAnswer: RAID 1
questions.explanation: RAID 1 mirrors all data across two or more disks. Every write goes to all mirrors. Capacity = disk_size (for 2-disk mirror). Can survive N-1 disk failures. Simplest redundancy.

questions.question: What is the minimum number of disks for RAID 6?
options: 2 through the built-in API with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
4 — need at least 2 data disks + 2 parity disks for dual-parity protection
3 by default behavior
1 under normal conditions with additional processing with typical engine sett
questions.correctAnswer: 4 — need at least 2 data disks + 2 parity disks for dual-parity protection
questions.explanation: RAID 6 uses dual parity (P+Q). Can survive 2 simultaneous disk failures. Minimum 4 disks. Capacity = (N-2) × disk_size. Write penalty: must update both parity blocks.

questions.question: What is the RAID write hole?
options: A physical defect as defined in the specification
When a crash occurs between data write and parity update — inconsistent state after reboot. ZFS and some hardware RAID use copy-on-write to avoid this.
A performance bug using standard engine approaches
A security vulnerability for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: When a crash occurs between data write and parity update — inconsistent state after reboot. ZFS and some hardware RAID use copy-on-write to avoid this.
questions.explanation: RAID 5 write = write data to one disk + update parity on another. If power fails between these, data and parity are inconsistent. Battery-backed cache or CoW prevents this.

questions.question: What is the primary benefit of DMA over programmed I/O?
options: Higher security within the current scope
Frees the CPU during data transfers
Lower latency using standard engine approaches
Better error handling
questions.correctAnswer: Frees the CPU during data transfers
questions.explanation: DMA allows peripherals to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU involvement per byte. CPU sets up the transfer and goes back to useful work. The DMA controller handles the actual data movement.

questions.question: What does the DMA controller need from the CPU before starting a transfer?
options: The data to transfer as defined in the specification
Source address, destination address, and transfer size
A list of free memory pages within the current scope
The CPU's permission every cycle
questions.correctAnswer: Source address, destination address, and transfer size
questions.explanation: CPU programs the DMA controller with: source address, destination address, transfer count, and direction (read/write). After setup, the DMA controller operates independently.

questions.question: What is bus mastering?
questions.correctAnswer: A device taking control of the memory bus to perform transfers independently
acceptableAnswers: A device controlling the bus for DMA
When a device takes control of the bus
device controls the bus for direct memory access
questions.explanation: Bus mastering: a peripheral device becomes the bus master, driving address and control lines to read/write memory directly. The CPU is temporarily disconnected from the bus during the transfer.

questions.question: What happens when DMA and CPU try to access memory simultaneously?
options: The system crashes across all supported platforms
The DMA controller always wins
Cycle stealing — DMA uses cycles the CPU doesn't need
They share the bus equally as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: Cycle stealing — DMA uses cycles the CPU doesn't need
questions.explanation: DMA uses 'cycle stealing': it transfers one word at a time during cycles when the CPU doesn't need the memory bus. The CPU is only stalled if both need memory simultaneously.

questions.question: What does an IOMMU do?
options: Encrypts DMA transfers under normal conditions with additional processing
Translates device virtual addresses to physical addresses
Speeds up DMA transfers for typical use cases
Compresses data during transfer using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Translates device virtual addresses to physical addresses
questions.explanation: IOMMU (I/O Memory Management Unit) provides address translation for DMA, similar to the MMU for CPU. Enables: device isolation (VM passthrough), scatter-gather, and protection against malicious DMA.

questions.question: What is the key difference between TCP and UDP?
options: TCP is faster through the built-in API with regular usag
TCP is connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery
UDP supports larger packets
TCP uses less memory by default behavior in most practi
questions.correctAnswer: TCP is connection-oriented with guaranteed delivery
questions.explanation: TCP: connection-oriented, guaranteed in-order delivery, flow control, congestion control. UDP: connectionless, no delivery guarantees, no ordering. TCP for reliability (web, file transfer), UDP for speed (games, VoIP).

questions.question: What is the three-way handshake in TCP?
questions.correctAnswer: SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK
questions.explanation: TCP connection setup: Client sends SYN with random sequence number. Server responds with SYN-ACK (acknowledging client's SYN, sending its own SYN). Client sends ACK. Connection established. Adds 1 RTT latency.

questions.question: What port range is available for user applications?
options: All ports are available
questions.explanation: Ports 0-1023: well-known (HTTP:80, SSH:22). 1024-49151: registered ports (can be used by applications). 49152-65535: dynamic/private ports (ephemeral, used for client connections).

questions.question: What does the TCP Nagle algorithm do?
options: Prioritizes ACK packets
Delays small packets to combine them
Encrypts the TCP stream in most practical
Increases the window size within the stand
questions.correctAnswer: Delays small packets to combine them
questions.explanation: Nagle's algorithm delays sending small segments until previous data is acknowledged OR enough data accumulates to fill a segment. Reduces overhead for many small writes. Can be disabled with TCP_NODELAY for low-latency apps (games).

questions.question: What socket call prepares a server to accept connections?
options: socket()
connect()
listen()
bind()
questions.correctAnswer: listen()
questions.explanation: Sequence: socket() → bind() → listen() → accept(). listen() marks the socket as passive and creates the backlog queue for incoming connections. accept() blocks until a client connects.

questions.question: What is the first code the CPU executes after power-on?
options: The OS kernel
The bootloader
Firmware (BIOS/UEFI)
The CPU's built-in self-test
questions.correctAnswer: Firmware (BIOS/UEFI)
questions.explanation: After power-on, the CPU fetches its first instruction from the reset vector (physical address 0xFFFFFFF0 on x86). This points to the firmware (BIOS/UEFI), which performs POST and hardware initialization.

questions.question: What does POST stand for?
questions.correctAnswer: Power-On Self-Test
questions.explanation: POST is the firmware's initial hardware check: CPU registers, memory (counting RAM), basic I/O devices. Failures are signaled via beep codes or LED patterns before any display output is available.

questions.question: What is the main difference between BIOS and UEFI?
options: BIOS is faster as defined in the specification with regular
UEFI supports larger disks and has a graphical interface
BIOS is more secure in most implementations
UEFI runs on ARM only as defined in the specification
questions.correctAnswer: UEFI supports larger disks and has a graphical interface
questions.explanation: BIOS: 16-bit real mode, MBR (max 2TB disks), text-only. UEFI: 32/64-bit, GPT (max 9.4 ZB), graphical interface, Secure Boot, network boot built-in. UEFI replaced BIOS as the standard around 2012.

questions.question: What does the bootloader do after firmware hands off control?
options: Initialize the GPU
Load the OS kernel into memory
Run user applications
Mount the filesystem permanently
questions.correctAnswer: Load the OS kernel into memory
questions.explanation: The bootloader (GRUB, Windows Boot Manager) loads the kernel image from disk into memory, sets up initial CPU state (protected/long mode), and jumps to the kernel's entry point. The kernel then takes over.

questions.question: What is the difference between MBR and GPT partitioning?
options: No difference for typical use cases
MBR: legacy, max 2TB disk, 4 primary partitions. GPT: modern, max 9.4ZB, 128 partitions, CRC checksum, backup header.
GPT is slower in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
MBR is for SSDs only by default behavior with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: MBR: legacy, max 2TB disk, 4 primary partitions. GPT: modern, max 9.4ZB, 128 partitions, CRC checksum, backup header.
questions.explanation: MBR (Master Boot Record) dates from 1983. GPT (GUID Partition Table) is part of UEFI spec. GPT stores backup partition table at end of disk for recovery.

questions.question: What does the bootloader load before the OS kernel?
options: Nothing under normal conditions
The initramfs/initrd — a temporary root filesystem with drivers needed to mount the real root (e.g., NVMe driver, LUKS encryption keys)
The desktop environment for typical use cases with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The web browser for typical use cases under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: The initramfs/initrd — a temporary root filesystem with drivers needed to mount the real root (e.g., NVMe driver, LUKS encryption keys)
questions.explanation: initramfs = compressed cpio archive loaded by bootloader. Contains kernel modules for disk controller, filesystem, encryption. Kernel mounts initramfs as root, loads modules, pivots to real root.

questions.question: How many threads are in an NVIDIA warp?
questions.explanation: NVIDIA GPUs execute threads in groups of 32 called warps. All threads in a warp execute the same instruction (SIMT — Single Instruction Multiple Thread). AMD's equivalent is a wavefront of 64 threads.

questions.question: What happens when threads in a warp take different branch paths?
options: The GPU crashes by default behavior
Branch divergence — both paths execute serially
Only one path executes through the built-in API
Threads vote on which path to take
questions.correctAnswer: Branch divergence — both paths execute serially
