questions.explanation: Branch divergence: when threads in a warp take different branches, the GPU serializes execution. First all threads taking path A execute (others masked), then path B executes. Performance penalty proportional to divergence.

questions.question: What is shared memory on a GPU?
options: Memory shared between GPU and CPU
On-chip SRAM shared between threads in a block
VRAM accessible by all SMs by default behavior
Memory shared between different GPUs
questions.correctAnswer: On-chip SRAM shared between threads in a block
questions.explanation: Shared memory is fast on-chip SRAM (~128KB per SM on RTX 4090) shared between all threads in a thread block. ~20-30x lower latency than VRAM. Critical for tiling, reductions, and cooperative algorithms.

questions.question: Why does a GPU have thousands of cores while a CPU has ~16?
options: GPUs are simpler to manufacture
GPUs are designed for throughput over latency
CPU cores are underutilized
It's just marketing — GPU cores are the same as CPU cores
questions.correctAnswer: GPUs are designed for throughput over latency
questions.explanation: CPUs optimize for single-thread latency (branch prediction, OoO execution, large caches). GPUs optimize for throughput: hide memory latency by switching between thousands of threads. A GPU 'core' is simpler than a CPU core.

questions.question: What is an NVIDIA warp?
options: A rendering technique under normal conditions with additional processing in most practical sc
A group of 32 threads executing in lockstep on one SM — same instruction, different data
A memory unit as defined in the specification with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of shader under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: A group of 32 threads executing in lockstep on one SM — same instruction, different data
questions.explanation: Warp = GPU's scheduling unit. 32 threads share one instruction fetch. Branch divergence within a warp serializes both paths. AMD equivalent: wavefront (64 threads).

questions.question: Why can't GPU threads diverge freely like CPU threads?
options: They can within the current scope
SIMT architecture — 32 threads share one instruction decoder. If half take branch A and half branch B, both execute serially while the other half is masked. Divergence costs performance.
GPU threads are slower as defined in the specification
GPU threads share registers via the core framework with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: SIMT architecture — 32 threads share one instruction decoder. If half take branch A and half branch B, both execute serially while the other half is masked. Divergence costs performance.
questions.explanation: GPU warps execute SIMT (Single Instruction Multiple Threads). All 32 threads must agree on which instruction to execute. Divergent threads = serialized execution = wasted hardware.

questions.question: What is the difference between an interrupt and an exception?
options: Interrupts are synchronous, exceptions are asynchronous with typical engin
Interrupts come from external hardware, exceptions come from the CPU
They are the same thing within the current scope
Interrupts are handled by the OS, exceptions by the CPU with typical e
questions.correctAnswer: Interrupts come from external hardware, exceptions come from the CPU
questions.explanation: Interrupts: asynchronous, from external hardware (keyboard, NIC, timer). Exceptions: synchronous, from CPU (division by zero, page fault, illegal instruction). Both use the interrupt descriptor table (IDT) for handling.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Interrupt Descriptor Table (IDT)?
options: To list all connected devices
To map interrupt vectors to handler functions
To track interrupt priorities in most practical scenar
To store interrupt statistics under normal operat
questions.correctAnswer: To map interrupt vectors to handler functions
questions.explanation: The IDT is a CPU data structure (256 entries on x86) that maps each interrupt/exception vector to its handler (ISR). The CPU indexes into the IDT when an interrupt occurs to find the correct handler address.

questions.question: Why should interrupt service routines (ISRs) be as short as possible?
options: To save memory via the core framework with additional processing
Interrupts are disabled during ISR execution
The CPU runs slower in ISR mode
ISRs have limited stack space
questions.correctAnswer: Interrupts are disabled during ISR execution
questions.explanation: During an ISR, interrupts of equal or lower priority are masked. Long ISRs delay other interrupts, causing missed deadlines (audio pops, network packet loss). Split work into: top-half (ISR, minimal) and bottom-half (deferred work).

questions.question: What does the APIC do?
options: Encrypts interrupt signals
Routes interrupts to specific CPU cores
Amplifies interrupt signals
Logs interrupt events in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: Routes interrupts to specific CPU cores
questions.explanation: The Advanced Programmable Interrupt Controller routes interrupts to specific cores, handles interrupt priority, and enables inter-processor interrupts (IPIs). The LAPIC (local) is per-core; the IO-APIC handles external devices.

questions.question: What is an interrupt storm?
questions.correctAnswer: A device generating interrupts faster than the CPU can handle them
questions.explanation: An interrupt storm: a device generates interrupts at a rate exceeding the CPU's ability to service them. The CPU spends 100% time in ISRs, starving user processes. Fixed by interrupt coalescing or rate limiting in the driver.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between VMs and containers?
options: No difference as defined in the specification
VMs each run their own OS kernel (hypervisor isolation); containers share the host kernel (namespace/cgroup isolation)
Containers are faster; VMs are more secure by default behavior within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
VMs use Docker; containers use Hyper-V across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: VMs each run their own OS kernel (hypervisor isolation); containers share the host kernel (namespace/cgroup isolation)
questions.explanation: VMs virtualize hardware — each runs a full OS. Containers share the host kernel, using namespaces for view isolation and cgroups for resource limits.

questions.question: What is a Type 1 hypervisor?
options: Runs as an app on a host OS under normal conditions with regular usage p
Runs directly on hardware (bare metal) — no host OS underneath
Runs inside a VM for typical use cases
Runs as a browser plugin by default behavior with regular usage patter
questions.correctAnswer: Runs directly on hardware (bare metal) — no host OS underneath
questions.explanation: Type 1 (ESXi, Hyper-V, KVM, Xen) runs on bare metal. Type 2 (VirtualBox, VMware Workstation) runs as an application on a host OS.

questions.question: What happens when a guest OS executes a privileged instruction in VMX non-root mode?
options: The instruction is silently ignored by default behavior with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A VM exit occurs — CPU saves guest state, loads hypervisor state, transitions to root mode for handling
The guest OS crashes by default behavior
The instruction executes normally via the core framework with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A VM exit occurs — CPU saves guest state, loads hypervisor state, transitions to root mode for handling
questions.explanation: Privileged instructions in non-root mode trigger VM exits. The hypervisor examines the exit reason, handles it, and resumes the guest with VMRESUME. Cost: ~500-1000 cycles.

questions.question: What do Extended Page Tables (EPT) provide?
options: Faster disk access under normal conditions for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A second layer of address translation: guest virtual → guest physical → host physical, allowing guest OS to manage its own page tables freely
More RAM for VMs via the core framework
Encrypted memory via the core framework for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A second layer of address translation: guest virtual → guest physical → host physical, allowing guest OS to manage its own page tables freely
questions.explanation: EPT adds a second translation layer managed by the hypervisor. Without EPT, every guest page table update causes a VM exit (shadow page tables). With EPT, guest manages its own PTs freely.

questions.question: What Linux kernel features do Docker containers use?
questions.correctAnswer: Namespaces (view isolation — PID, NET, MNT, UTS, IPC) and cgroups (resource limits — CPU shares, memory limits, blkio throttling)
questions.explanation: Namespaces limit what a container can SEE: its own PID tree, network interfaces, filesystem root. Cgroups limit what it can USE: CPU share, memory cap, I/O bandwidth.

questions.question: What is the 'noisy neighbor' problem in virtualization?
options: Loud fans by default behavior
One VM's workload degrades other VMs on the same physical host via shared resource contention (CPU steal, cache pollution, memory bandwidth)
VMs that make noise via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Network interference only across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: One VM's workload degrades other VMs on the same physical host via shared resource contention (CPU steal, cache pollution, memory bandwidth)
questions.explanation: Multi-tenant hosts share CPU caches, memory bandwidth, and I/O. A noisy neighbor doing heavy work can degrade VMs on the same machine. Dedicated servers avoid this.

questions.question: Why do drivers run in kernel space?
options: It's faster to write them there using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
They need direct hardware access — kernel space has full privileges to talk to physical devices
They don't — all drivers run in user space across all supported platforms with typical engine se
For security reasons using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: They need direct hardware access — kernel space has full privileges to talk to physical devices
questions.explanation: Drivers communicate directly with hardware (GPU registers, NVMe controller, network card DMA). This requires ring 0 privilege which only kernel space has.

questions.question: What happens when a kernel-space driver crashes?
options: Only the driver restarts through the built-in API
The entire system crashes (BSOD/kernel panic) — there's no protection boundary
The application using it crashes using standard engine approaches under normal op
Nothing — drivers are self-healing using standard engine approaches with typical engi
questions.correctAnswer: The entire system crashes (BSOD/kernel panic) — there's no protection boundary
questions.explanation: Kernel code runs with full privilege. A buggy driver writing to the wrong address corrupts kernel memory → system crash. User-space drivers (UMD) sacrifice some performance for crash isolation.

questions.question: What is the difference between a UMD and KMD in GPU drivers?
options: No difference as defined in the specification
UMD (User Mode Driver) runs in user space handling shader compilation; KMD (Kernel Mode Driver) runs in kernel space handling memory allocation and command submission
UMD is for AMD; KMD is for NVIDIA through the built-in API
KMD is deprecated via the core framework with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: UMD (User Mode Driver) runs in user space handling shader compilation; KMD (Kernel Mode Driver) runs in kernel space handling memory allocation and command submission
questions.explanation: D3D12/Vulkan have UMD for shader compilation and KMD for VRAM management. UMD crash = application crash. KMD crash = BSOD.

questions.question: How does a userspace application communicate with a driver?
options: Direct function calls by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Via the VFS layer and syscalls — the kernel routes the request through the appropriate driver
Shared memory only under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Email using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Via the VFS layer and syscalls — the kernel routes the request through the appropriate driver
questions.explanation: User space → syscall → VFS/generic layer → driver → hardware. The kernel acts as intermediary, translating generic requests to driver-specific operations.

questions.question: What is a kernel module?
questions.correctAnswer: A piece of code that can be loaded/unloaded into the running kernel dynamically — adds driver support without recompiling the kernel
questions.explanation: Kernel modules (.ko on Linux, .sys on Windows) extend the kernel at runtime. Load with insmod/modprobe. Unload with rmmod. No reboot needed to add driver support.

questions.question: What CPU optimization do Spectre and Meltdown exploit?
options: Hyper-Threading within the current scope
Speculative execution — the CPU executes instructions ahead of time based on branch prediction, leaving microarchitectural side effects even when the speculation is wrong
Turbo Boost by default behavior
SIMD by default behavior with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Speculative execution — the CPU executes instructions ahead of time based on branch prediction, leaving microarchitectural side effects even when the speculation is wrong
questions.explanation: The CPU speculates past branches and security checks. When wrong, architectural state is rolled back but cache state is NOT. Attackers measure cache timing to infer secret data.

questions.question: What side channel does Spectre v1 (bounds check bypass) use?
options: Power consumption across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Cache timing — the attacker trains the branch predictor, provides an out-of-bounds index, and measures which cache line was loaded speculatively
Network latency in most implementations
Disk I/O using standard engine approaches with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cache timing — the attacker trains the branch predictor, provides an out-of-bounds index, and measures which cache line was loaded speculatively
questions.explanation: Spectre v1: mistrain branch predictor so CPU speculates past array bounds check. The speculatively accessed memory loads a cache line. Attacker times cache access to determine the secret value.

questions.question: What is the difference between Spectre and Meltdown?
options: No difference for typical use cases
Spectre: cross-process/same-privilege using branch prediction. Meltdown: user space reads kernel memory by exploiting out-of-order execution before privilege check completes
Spectre is newer across all supported platforms
Meltdown only affects AMD for typical use cases with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Spectre: cross-process/same-privilege using branch prediction. Meltdown: user space reads kernel memory by exploiting out-of-order execution before privilege check completes
questions.explanation: Meltdown: CPU executes instructions OoO, accessing kernel memory BEFORE the privilege check. Cache state persists after the fault. Spectre: branch predictor manipulation. Different mechanisms, same result.

questions.question: What is KPTI (Kernel Page Table Isolation)?
options: A performance optimization in most implementations in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Meltdown mitigation — kernel pages are unmapped from userspace page tables. Every syscall switches page tables, adding ~100-500 cycle overhead
A new CPU feature via the core framework
A hypervisor feature through the built-in API with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Meltdown mitigation — kernel pages are unmapped from userspace page tables. Every syscall switches page tables, adding ~100-500 cycle overhead
questions.explanation: KPTI gives the kernel its own page table. User-space can't access kernel memory even speculatively. Tradeoff: every syscall needs a PT switch → performance cost.

questions.question: Why is disabling Hyper-Threading recommended for security-sensitive workloads?
options: It's not across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
SMT threads share L1 cache — one thread can observe the other's cache state, enabling cross-thread side-channel attacks
It saves power via the core framework
For performance as defined in the specification with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: SMT threads share L1 cache — one thread can observe the other's cache state, enabling cross-thread side-channel attacks
questions.explanation: Two threads on one core share L1/L2 cache. Thread A can measure cache timing to infer what Thread B accessed. Disable SMT to eliminate this attack surface.

questions.question: What are Coffman's four necessary conditions for deadlock?
questions.correctAnswer: Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
acceptableAnswers: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
mutual exclusion hold and wait no preemption circular wait
questions.explanation: All four must hold for deadlock to occur. Break any one to prevent/recover from deadlock. Lock ordering breaks circular wait; try_lock breaks hold-and-wait.

questions.question: How does consistent lock ordering prevent deadlock?
options: It makes locks faster for typical use cases
If every thread acquires locks in the same global order (always A before B), circular wait is impossible
It uses fewer locks under normal conditions with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It's a debugging tool for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: If every thread acquires locks in the same global order (always A before B), circular wait is impossible
questions.explanation: Lock ordering eliminates the circular wait condition. Thread 1: lock(A), lock(B). Thread 2: lock(A), lock(B) — same order, no cycle possible.

questions.question: What is the hold-and-wait condition?
options: A thread holds no locks through the built-in API
A thread holds at least one resource while waiting to acquire additional resources
A thread waits without holding under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxx
A thread releases all locks before waiting in most implementations in standard config
questions.correctAnswer: A thread holds at least one resource while waiting to acquire additional resources
questions.explanation: Thread holds lock A while waiting for lock B. If Thread B holds lock B while waiting for lock A — deadlock. Break this by acquiring all locks atomically or releasing held locks before waiting.

questions.question: What is the difference between deadlock and livelock?
options: They're the same as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deadlock: threads are blocked (sleeping). Livelock: threads are actively changing state but making zero net progress — like two people trying to pass in a corridor
Livelock is faster as defined in the specification in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Deadlock is recoverable for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Deadlock: threads are blocked (sleeping). Livelock: threads are actively changing state but making zero net progress — like two people trying to pass in a corridor
questions.explanation: Deadlock = blocked in FUTEX_WAIT, consuming no CPU. Livelock = threads are RUNNABLE, executing CAS loops, but yield to each other endlessly — burning CPU, zero progress.

questions.question: What is starvation in the context of locking?
options: A deadlock variant through the built-in API
A thread is ready to run but is perpetually denied the resource because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
A performance problem using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of livelock through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A thread is ready to run but is perpetually denied the resource because higher-priority threads keep acquiring it
questions.explanation: Starvation: a low-priority thread never gets the lock. Fix with fair queuing (ticket lock) or priority inheritance.

questions.question: What is the Multi-Level Feedback Queue (MLFQ)?
options: A single queue in most implementations with additional processing with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A scheduler with multiple priority queues — jobs move between queues based on observed behavior (CPU-bound drop, I/O-bound rise)
A hardware queue across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A networking scheduler by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: A scheduler with multiple priority queues — jobs move between queues based on observed behavior (CPU-bound drop, I/O-bound rise)
questions.explanation: MLFQ learns: short/I/O jobs get high priority. Long CPU jobs get demoted to lower priority with longer time slices. Balances responsiveness with throughput.

questions.question: How does MLFQ prevent starvation of low-priority jobs?
options: It doesn't via the core framework
Priority boost — periodically move all jobs to the top queue, giving everyone a chance
It kills low-priority jobs through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It uses more cores by default behavior within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Priority boost — periodically move all jobs to the top queue, giving everyone a chance
questions.explanation: Periodic priority boost prevents indefinite starvation. All jobs temporarily get highest priority, ensuring long-running CPU jobs eventually get CPU time.

questions.question: What is proportional share scheduling?
options: Equal shares for all under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Each process gets a guaranteed fraction of CPU time — e.g., Process A gets 2x more than B via lottery or stride scheduling
First-come-first-serve within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Round-robin only through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Each process gets a guaranteed fraction of CPU time — e.g., Process A gets 2x more than B via lottery or stride scheduling
questions.explanation: Lottery: each process holds tickets. Scheduler randomly draws — more tickets = more wins. Stride: deterministic, each process has a stride value — smallest stride runs next.

questions.question: What is the Linux CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler)?
options: A lottery scheduler using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A proportional share scheduler using virtual runtime (vruntime) — the thread with the lowest vruntime runs next
A MLFQ variant as defined in the specification
A real-time scheduler as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A proportional share scheduler using virtual runtime (vruntime) — the thread with the lowest vruntime runs next
questions.explanation: CFS tracks vruntime per thread (actual runtime weighted by priority). Red-black tree ordered by vruntime. Pick leftmost (minimum vruntime) to run. Perfect fairness over time.

questions.question: What scheduling class do real-time threads use on Linux?
options: CFS within the current scope with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxx
SCHED_FIFO or SCHED_RR — always preempts CFS threads, provides deterministic scheduling
SCHED_BATCH by default behavior
SCHED_IDLE for typical use cases with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: SCHED_FIFO or SCHED_RR — always preempts CFS threads, provides deterministic scheduling
questions.explanation: Real-time classes have static priority above CFS (nice -20 to +19). SCHED_FIFO: runs until it yields. SCHED_RR: round-robin with time slice. Always chosen before CFS threads.

questions.question: What does the perspective projection matrix do?
options: Translates vertices for typical use cases
Maps 3D view-space coordinates to clip space — divides x and y by z (distance), creating perspective foreshortening
Rotates the camera across all supported platforms for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Lighting calculation within the current scope for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Maps 3D view-space coordinates to clip space — divides x and y by z (distance), creating perspective foreshortening
questions.explanation: Perspective matrix stores z in the w component. After vertex shader, the GPU performs the homogeneous divide (x/w, y/w, z/w) — this creates perspective: far objects appear smaller.

questions.question: What is the homogeneous divide (perspective divide)?
options: A vertex shader operation via the core framework within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
GPU hardware step: divides xyz by w after the vertex shader — converts from homogeneous clip space to NDC
A pixel shader under normal conditions
A CPU operation using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: GPU hardware step: divides xyz by w after the vertex shader — converts from homogeneous clip space to NDC
questions.explanation: After the vertex shader outputs clip-space coordinates, the GPU's fixed-function hardware performs x/w, y/w, z/w. This is NOT programmable — it always happens.

questions.question: Why does the projection matrix store z in the w component?
options: It doesn't within the current scope
To enable perspective-correct interpolation — w contains the original view-space depth, used to interpolate texture coordinates correctly in screen space
For debugging via the core framework
For lighting in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: To enable perspective-correct interpolation — w contains the original view-space depth, used to interpolate texture coordinates correctly in screen space
questions.explanation: Screen-space interpolation of triangle attributes is NOT linear in 3D. Dividing by w (perspective-correct interpolation) fixes this — textures don't warp on triangles viewed at an angle.

questions.question: What is clip space and what happens there?
options: The final screen for typical use cases
A coordinate space where vertices are tested against the view frustum — triangles partially outside the frustum are clipped, generating new vertices at the boundary
Camera space via the core framework with additional processing
World space within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: A coordinate space where vertices are tested against the view frustum — triangles partially outside the frustum are clipped, generating new vertices at the boundary
questions.explanation: In clip space (-w ≤ x,y,z ≤ w), the GPU clips triangles against the 6 frustum planes. Triangles fully inside pass through. Partially visible triangles generate new edge vertices.

questions.question: What is reversed-Z and why is it used?
options: The Z-axis is flipped across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Far plane mapped to 0, near plane to 1 — floating point precision is denser near 0, giving better depth precision for distant objects
A rendering technique under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A camera setting in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: Far plane mapped to 0, near plane to 1 — floating point precision is denser near 0, giving better depth precision for distant objects
questions.explanation: Standard mapping: near=0, far=1 wastes precision close to camera where it's less needed. Reversed-Z uses FP's natural precision distribution (dense near 0) for far objects where Z-fighting occurs. UE5 uses reversed-Z.

questions.question: What are barycentric coordinates?
options: Pixel coordinates via the core framework across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Three weights (α,β,γ) that express a point inside a triangle as a weighted sum of its vertices — α+β+γ=1, all ≥0 for interior points
UV coordinates through the built-in API
Screen coordinates across all supported platforms across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Three weights (α,β,γ) that express a point inside a triangle as a weighted sum of its vertices — α+β+γ=1, all ≥0 for interior points
questions.explanation: P = α·A + β·B + γ·C. At vertex A: (1,0,0). At centroid: (1/3,1/3,1/3). Used to interpolate any vertex attribute (color, UV, normal) across the triangle surface.

questions.question: How does the GPU rasterizer compute barycentric coordinates?
options: It doesn't under normal conditions
Edge functions — for each pixel, compute signed distances to three edges. Normalize to get (α,β,γ).
Ray tracing using standard engine approaches within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Matrix inversion through the built-in API in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Edge functions — for each pixel, compute signed distances to three edges. Normalize to get (α,β,γ).
questions.explanation: GPU computes E01, E12, E20 edge functions for a pixel. Normalize by triangle area. These ARE the barycentric weights. Extremely efficient — a few MADs per pixel.

questions.question: Why does perspective-correct interpolation need barycentrics divided by w?
options: It doesn't through the built-in API
Screen-space barycentrics are NOT the same as 3D barycentrics due to perspective distortion. Dividing by the interpolated 1/w fixes this.
For performance under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For anti-aliasing within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Screen-space barycentrics are NOT the same as 3D barycentrics due to perspective distortion. Dividing by the interpolated 1/w fixes this.
questions.explanation: Without perspective correction: floor textures warp (affine texture mapping, PS1 style). With it: correct perspective. GPU computes α/z, β/z, γ/z and 1/z, then divides to get correct 3D barycentrics.

questions.question: Where on the triangle does α=1?
options: The centroid
Vertex A
The midpoint of BC
Outside the triangle
questions.correctAnswer: Vertex A
questions.explanation: At vertex A: barycentrics are (1,0,0). At B: (0,1,0). At C: (0,0,1). Each vertex has 1 for its own weight and 0 for others.

questions.question: What is the interior test using barycentrics?
options: Check distance to center within the current scope
All three barycentric coordinates must be ≥ 0 and ≤ 1
Check color value in most implementations with additional processing
Check depth under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: All three barycentric coordinates must be ≥ 0 and ≤ 1
questions.explanation: If any barycentric is negative, the point lies outside the triangle. α,β,γ ≥ 0 AND α+β+γ=1 means point is inside or on edge. This is how the GPU determines pixel coverage.

questions.question: What does BRDF stand for and what does it model?
questions.correctAnswer: Bidirectional Reflectance Distribution Function — models how light reflects off a surface: given incoming and outgoing directions, returns the ratio of reflected radiance
questions.explanation: BRDF(ωi, ωo) = dLo(ωo) / dEi(ωi). Physically based: must be reciprocal f(ωi,ωo)=f(ωo,ωi) and energy conserving ∫f·cosθ dω ≤ 1.

questions.question: What three terms compose the Cook-Torrance microfacet BRDF?
questions.correctAnswer: Normal Distribution Function (D — roughness), Geometric Shadowing (G — self-shadowing), Fresnel (F — angle-dependent reflectance)
questions.explanation: Cook-Torrance = D·G·F / (4·(n·l)·(n·v)). D= GGX/Trowbridge-Reitz. G= Smith. F= Schlick approximation. Physically based — used in UE5, Unity HDRP.

questions.question: What does the GGX normal distribution function model?
options: Light color by default behavior
Microfacet distribution — how many microfacets are aligned with the half-vector at given roughness
Shadow by default behavior with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Specular color as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Microfacet distribution — how many microfacets are aligned with the half-vector at given roughness
questions.explanation: GGX gives the long specular tail seen in real materials. Roughness=0: sharp mirror. Roughness=1: diffuse-like. Used universally in PBR.

questions.question: What is the Fresnel effect?
options: Light absorption in most implementations with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reflectance increases at grazing angles — water looks reflective at the horizon but transparent looking straight down
Diffuse lighting via the core framework
Shadow mapping within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reflectance increases at grazing angles — water looks reflective at the horizon but transparent looking straight down
questions.explanation: Schlick approximation: F = F0 + (1-F0)·(1-cosθ)⁵. F0 = reflectance at normal incidence (~0.04 for non-metals, tinted for metals). At 90°, ALL materials become fully reflective.

questions.question: What is the difference between metallic and dielectric (non-metal) F0 values?
options: No difference through the built-in API
Dielectrics: F0 ~0.04 (grayscale). Metals: F0 is tinted (gold=orange, copper=reddish) and much higher (0.5-1.0)
Metals are darker within the current scope across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Dielectrics are always white for typical use cases with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Dielectrics: F0 ~0.04 (grayscale). Metals: F0 is tinted (gold=orange, copper=reddish) and much higher (0.5-1.0)
questions.explanation: Metalness workflow: metal=0 → F0=0.04 (dielectric). metal=1 → F0=BaseColor (metal — no diffuse, all color comes from specular). This is the core of PBR texturing.

questions.question: What do spherical harmonics encode?
options: Vertex positions through the built-in API
Lighting information on a sphere — direction-dependent irradiance for diffuse global illumination
Texture coordinates through the built-in API for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Mesh topology for typical use cases with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Lighting information on a sphere — direction-dependent irradiance for diffuse global illumination
questions.explanation: SH encodes functions on a sphere using basis functions (Y_lm). For diffuse GI, 9 coefficients (3 bands, l≤2) capture ~99% of lighting energy.

questions.question: How many SH coefficients encode diffuse indirect lighting?
options: 1 via the core framework with additional processing
64 in most implementations
Unlimited
questions.explanation: Diffuse lighting is low-frequency — 3 bands (9 coefficients) capture >99% of the signal. Each coefficient is an RGB vector. UE's SkyLight uses 3rd-order SH.

questions.question: What does the l=0 (first band) SH coefficient represent?
options: Directional light through the built-in API within the standard framework
The average (DC) ambient light level — a constant value over the sphere
Shadow information using standard engine approaches in standard configurati
Specular highlights for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: The average (DC) ambient light level — a constant value over the sphere
questions.explanation: Y_00 = constant. The l=0 coefficient is the mean irradiance — the ambient base level. Higher bands add directional variation.

questions.question: How are SH coefficients used in a shader?
options: They're not for typical use cases
Reconstruct irradiance: dot the SH coefficients with the basis functions evaluated at the surface normal direction
For texture sampling through the built-in API with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For vertex animation via the core framework in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reconstruct irradiance: dot the SH coefficients with the basis functions evaluated at the surface normal direction
questions.explanation: At runtime: evaluate Y_lm(normal) → dot with stored coefficients → get irradiance in that direction. 9 dot products per pixel.

questions.question: What is the convolution property that makes SH efficient for diffuse lighting?
options: Nothing special by default behavior
Convolution of the light probe with a Lambertian BRDF in SH space is just scaling the coefficients — a dot product at runtime
It's faster to render across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
It uses GPU hardware across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Convolution of the light probe with a Lambertian BRDF in SH space is just scaling the coefficients — a dot product at runtime
questions.explanation: SH convolution theorem: the SH coefficients of the convolution are the product of the coefficients of each function (times a diagonal matrix). This makes irradiance computation extremely cheap.

questions.question: How are SH light probes baked in Unreal Engine?
options: Manually placed through the built-in API with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Lightmass or GPU Lightmass samples the scene from probe positions, projects the incoming radiance onto SH basis functions, and stores the coefficients
At runtime during gameplay using standard engine approaches for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
They're hand-authored through the built-in API
questions.correctAnswer: Lightmass or GPU Lightmass samples the scene from probe positions, projects the incoming radiance onto SH basis functions, and stores the coefficients
questions.explanation: UE5: Place Lightmass Importance Volumes. Lightmass traces rays from probe positions to sample scene radiance. Projects onto 3rd-order SH (9 coefficients). Stored per-probe. Interpolated at runtime.

questions.question: What is the fundamental principle of Monte Carlo integration?
options: Analytic solutions within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxx
Estimate integrals by averaging random samples: ∫f(x)dx ≈ (1/N)·Σ f(x_i)/p(x_i)
Precomputed tables within the current scope with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxx
Finite differences under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Estimate integrals by averaging random samples: ∫f(x)dx ≈ (1/N)·Σ f(x_i)/p(x_i)
questions.explanation: MC integration: draw N random samples from distribution p(x), evaluate integrand f(x)/p(x) at each, average. Converges at O(1/√N). Unbiased.

questions.question: What does importance sampling improve?
options: Nothing using standard engine approaches with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Reduces variance by drawing more samples where the integrand is large — matches sampling distribution to the function
Speed only for typical use cases
Memory usage in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Reduces variance by drawing more samples where the integrand is large — matches sampling distribution to the function
questions.explanation: Sampling uniformly wastes samples on dark regions. Importance sampling concentrates samples on bright regions (lights, specular lobes).

questions.question: What is Russian roulette in path tracing?
options: A game across all supported platforms
Probabilistically terminating paths: with probability p, continue; with probability 1-p, stop and divide by p. Unbiased, limits infinite recursion.
A Russian game as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of noise within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Probabilistically terminating paths: with probability p, continue; with probability 1-p, stop and divide by p. Unbiased, limits infinite recursion.
questions.explanation: Without RR, path length is unbounded. RR terminates paths randomly while preserving the expected value (divide surviving paths by p).

questions.question: What is the rendering equation?
questions.correctAnswer: L_o = L_e + ∫ f_r·L_i·(n·ω_i)·dω_i — outgoing light = emitted + reflected (BRDF × incident light × cosine term integrated over hemisphere)
acceptableAnswers: L_o = L_e + integral of BRDF * L_i * cos
questions.explanation: Kajiya 1986. L_o = emitted + reflected. Reflected = integral over hemisphere of BRDF × incoming radiance × cosine of incident angle. Path tracing solves this recursively.

questions.question: What is the difference between path tracing and ray tracing?
options: Same in most implementations
Ray tracing: single ray → intersection query. Path tracing: recursive ray bounces through the scene, accumulating light at each bounce following the rendering equation.
Path tracing is faster via the core framework with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Ray tracing is deprecated via the core framework with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Ray tracing: single ray → intersection query. Path tracing: recursive ray bounces through the scene, accumulating light at each bounce following the rendering equation.
questions.explanation: Ray trace = TraceRay() → hit result. Path trace = TraceRay() → shade → spawn more rays recursively → accumulate result. Path tracing solves the rendering equation; ray tracing is the building block.

questions.question: What is the typical number of samples per pixel for a clean path-traced image?
options: 1 by default behavior
1-16 for real-time (denoised), 100-1000+ for offline quality (no denoiser). Denoising (OptiX, DLSS RR) dramatically reduces required samples.
10,000 minimum via the core framework with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Always 1 through the built-in API within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: 1-16 for real-time (denoised), 100-1000+ for offline quality (no denoiser). Denoising (OptiX, DLSS RR) dramatically reduces required samples.
questions.explanation: Without denoising: hundreds to thousands of SPP. With temporal accumulation + DLSS Ray Reconstruction: 1-4 SPP can produce clean images. UE Lumen uses ~4 SPP with denoising.

questions.question: What is the difference between sRGB and linear color space?
options: No difference in most implementations for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
sRGB is gamma-encoded (~γ=2.2) for perceptual uniformity and efficient 8-bit storage; linear is physically correct for light calculations
sRGB is faster for typical use cases
Linear is deprecated by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: sRGB is gamma-encoded (~γ=2.2) for perceptual uniformity and efficient 8-bit storage; linear is physically correct for light calculations
questions.explanation: Light is linear (2× photons = 2× brightness). sRGB applies gamma for perceptual encoding (more bits in darks). Shading MUST happen in linear space; convert sRGB→linear before and linear→sRGB after.

questions.question: Why does HDR rendering matter?
options: It's just brighter under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Preserves light values >1.0 for bloom, tone mapping, and eye adaptation — without HDR, bright areas clip to white
It's faster for typical use cases
It uses less memory as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Preserves light values >1.0 for bloom, tone mapping, and eye adaptation — without HDR, bright areas clip to white
questions.explanation: HDR stores radiance values beyond [0,1]. Tone mapping compresses HDR→LDR for display. Bloom extracts bright regions. Without HDR, fire, sun, and bright lights all clip to pure white.

questions.question: What is ACES tone mapping?
options: A color space only by default behavior with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Academy Color Encoding System — industry-standard HDR→display transform with cinematic color response and desaturation of brights
A file format within the current scope
A GPU feature within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Academy Color Encoding System — industry-standard HDR→display transform with cinematic color response and desaturation of brights
questions.explanation: ACES is the film industry standard. Maps HDR scene radiance to display colors with realistic highlight roll-off and color accuracy. Used in UE5 by default.

questions.question: What is the visible color gamut of sRGB vs Rec.2020?
options: Same via the core framework
sRGB: ~35% of visible colors (narrow). Rec.2020: ~75% (wide gamut). HDR displays use Rec.2020 or DCI-P3 (~50%).
Rec.2020 is smaller for typical use cases in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
sRGB is wider using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: sRGB: ~35% of visible colors (narrow). Rec.2020: ~75% (wide gamut). HDR displays use Rec.2020 or DCI-P3 (~50%).
questions.explanation: sRGB is adequate for SDR but clips saturated colors. DCI-P3 captures more reds/greens. Rec.2020 captures most visible colors. UE5 supports HDR output with wide gamut.

questions.question: What does UE's eye adaptation (auto-exposure) do?
options: Nothing under normal conditions across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Simulates human eye adaptation — brightens dark areas and darkens bright areas over time. Uses histogram of scene luminance.
Film grain within the current scope under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Bloom in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: Simulates human eye adaptation — brightens dark areas and darkens bright areas over time. Uses histogram of scene luminance.
questions.explanation: Eye adaptation: measure scene average luminance, compute exposure value, interpolate over time (~2-5 second adaptation). Moving from dark cave to bright outdoors → smooth brightness transition.

questions.question: What is a signed distance field?
options: A texture under normal conditions
A function f(x) returning the distance to the nearest surface, with negative values inside the object
A rendering technique only by default behavior in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of shader under normal conditions with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: A function f(x) returning the distance to the nearest surface, with negative values inside the object
questions.explanation: SDF: f(p) < 0 inside, = 0 on surface, > 0 outside. |f(p)| = distance to nearest surface. Enables ray marching, smooth blending, and CSG operations.

questions.question: How does SDF ray marching work?
options: Step by fixed amount within the current scope with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
March along ray by the SDF value at current point — guaranteed not to overshoot, adapts step size
Binary search via the core framework
Random steps for typical use cases with additional processing with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: March along ray by the SDF value at current point — guaranteed not to overshoot, adapts step size
questions.explanation: SDF ray marching: step distance = f(point). Since f is the minimum distance to any surface, stepping by f guarantees you won't cross a surface. Converges fast near surfaces.

questions.question: What is smooth min (smin) used for in SDF modeling?
options: Nothing as defined in the specification in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Blends two SDFs smoothly — creates organic connection between shapes without sharp edges
Boolean operations by default behavior with additional processing with typical engine set
Ray marching under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Blends two SDFs smoothly — creates organic connection between shapes without sharp edges
questions.explanation: smin(a, b, k) = -log(exp(-k·a) + exp(-k·b))/k. Blends the minimum of two SDFs. k controls blend radius. Creates metaball-like merging.

questions.question: What is the SDF representation of a sphere?
options: A texture under normal conditions in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
f(p) = distance(p, center) - radius. Negative inside, zero on surface, positive outside.
A mesh for typical use cases
A function call by default behavior with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: f(p) = distance(p, center) - radius. Negative inside, zero on surface, positive outside.
questions.explanation: Sphere SDF: |p - C| - R. Box SDF: max(|p-C| - 0.5*Size). Simple primitives combine via min (union), max (intersection), -f (subtract) to build complex shapes.

questions.question: How does UE use distance fields for GPU particles?
options: They don't as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Distance field collision: particles query the global distance field for collision response — cheaper than per-particle mesh collision
For rendering only under normal conditions with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
For audio in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: Distance field collision: particles query the global distance field for collision response — cheaper than per-particle mesh collision
questions.explanation: UE generates a Global Distance Field for static geometry. GPU particles sample the distance field to collide with the world. Much cheaper than mesh-vs-mesh collision for thousands of particles.

questions.question: What is FVector in Unreal Engine?
options: A string type under normal conditions
A 3D vector (float X, Y, Z) — position, direction, velocity. Core math type.
A rotation type through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A color type within the current scope with additional processing across common plat
questions.correctAnswer: A 3D vector (float X, Y, Z) — position, direction, velocity. Core math type.
questions.explanation: FVector is a 12-byte struct (3 floats). Used everywhere: Actor location, velocity, surface normals, direction vectors. Equivalent to glm::vec3 or XMFLOAT3.

questions.question: What does FRotator store?
options: A quaternion via the core framework
Euler angles (Pitch, Yaw, Roll) in degrees — intuitive but suffers from gimbal lock
A matrix across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A vector using standard engine approaches with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Euler angles (Pitch, Yaw, Roll) in degrees — intuitive but suffers from gimbal lock
questions.explanation: FRotator = (Pitch, Yaw, Roll) in degrees. Used in Blueprints and editor for human readability. Internally converted to FQuat for rotation math. Avoid for interpolation.

questions.question: Why use FQuat instead of FRotator for rotation?
options: FRotator is deprecated by default behavior
FQuat avoids gimbal lock, enables smooth interpolation (SLERP), and composes rotations correctly
FQuat is smaller as defined in the specification with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FQuat is faster to render by default behavior across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FQuat avoids gimbal lock, enables smooth interpolation (SLERP), and composes rotations correctly
questions.explanation: FQuat = 4 floats (x,y,z,w). No gimbal lock. SLERP provides constant angular velocity. FRotator is for human input; FQuat is for math.

questions.question: What does FVector::DotProduct compute?
options: Cross product through the built-in API across common platformsxxxx
|A||B|cos(θ) — projection of A onto B. Zero when perpendicular.
Distance in most implementations with additional processing
Angle in degrees by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: |A||B|cos(θ) — projection of A onto B. Zero when perpendicular.
questions.explanation: A·B > 0: acute angle. = 0: perpendicular. < 0: obtuse. Used for: view direction test (DotProduct(Forward, ToTarget)), lighting (N·L).

questions.question: What does FVector::Normalize() do?
options: Makes the vector zero as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkx
Scales the vector to unit length (magnitude = 1) while preserving direction
Rotates the vector for typical use cases
Projects onto another vector via the core framework within the standard frameworkx
questions.correctAnswer: Scales the vector to unit length (magnitude = 1) while preserving direction
questions.explanation: Normalize() returns vector/length. FVector::GetSafeNormal() returns unit vector or zero vector if input is degenerate. Normalization is not free — sqrt is expensive.

questions.question: What is the quaternion double-cover problem?
options: Memory overhead in most implementations across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Q and -Q represent the SAME rotation. Before SLERP, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take the shorter arc on the hypersphere.
Speed issue through the built-in API across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Precision issue by default behavior
questions.correctAnswer: Q and -Q represent the SAME rotation. Before SLERP, check dot product — if negative, negate one to take the shorter arc on the hypersphere.
questions.explanation: Quaternions double-cover SO(3). q and -q produce identical 3D rotations. Without correction, SLERP might interpolate the long way around (359° rotation instead of 1°).

questions.question: What does FTransform store?
options: Only rotation by default behavior
Rotation (FQuat), Translation (FVector), and Scale3D (FVector) — the complete rigid-body transform
Only position within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A 4x4 matrix directly in most implementations within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Rotation (FQuat), Translation (FVector), and Scale3D (FVector) — the complete rigid-body transform
questions.explanation: FTransform decomposes transforms: Rotation (quaternion, no gimbal lock), Translation (vector), Scale (vector). More efficient than storing a full 4x4 matrix.

questions.question: How is a 4x4 matrix constructed from an FTransform?
options: It can't in most implementations
Scale matrix · Rotation matrix · Translation matrix — composed in that order (SRT)
Rotation only across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Direct component storage using standard engine approaches within the standard framew
questions.correctAnswer: Scale matrix · Rotation matrix · Translation matrix — composed in that order (SRT)
questions.explanation: SRT order: Scale → Rotate → Translate. The quaternion converts to 3x3 rotation, embedded in 4x4 with translation. ToMatrixWithScale() produces the final transform.

questions.question: What is the relationship between FTransform and FMatrix in UE?
options: No relationship under normal conditions
FTransform is the decomposed storage; FMatrix is the 4x4 computation-ready form. Convert with ToMatrixWithScale()
They're the same across all supported platforms with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FTransform is deprecated within the current scope with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FTransform is the decomposed storage; FMatrix is the 4x4 computation-ready form. Convert with ToMatrixWithScale()
questions.explanation: FTransform for storage (no redundancy, no shear). FMatrix for GPU (shaders need 4x4). Convert at CPU→GPU boundary.

questions.question: What is TransformPosition vs TransformVector?
options: Same through the built-in API
TransformPosition applies translation (for points). TransformVector ignores translation (for directions/normals). Both apply rotation and scale.
Position is faster through the built-in API with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Vector is deprecated as defined in the specification within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: TransformPosition applies translation (for points). TransformVector ignores translation (for directions/normals). Both apply rotation and scale.
questions.explanation: FTransform::TransformPosition(Pos): T * R * S * Pos (includes translation). TransformVector(Vec): R * S * Vec (no translation — vectors can't be translated, they're directions).

questions.question: What does FTransform::GetRelativeTransform do?
options: Nothing under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Converts a world-space transform to be relative to another transform — GetRelativeTransform(Parent, Child) = Parent⁻¹ * Child
Rotates via the core framework
Scales within the current scope with additional processing within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Converts a world-space transform to be relative to another transform — GetRelativeTransform(Parent, Child) = Parent⁻¹ * Child
questions.explanation: Relative transform: transform child into parent's local space. Parent.Inverse() * Child. Used for: getting a weapon's transform relative to the character's hand.

questions.question: Why is FTransform preferred over FMatrix for object transforms?
options: It's faster to render in most implementations with additional processing across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
FTransform enforces valid decomposition (no shear, uniform scale or separate axes). FMatrix can accumulate numerical error and non-rigid deformations.
FMatrix is deprecated under normal conditions
FTransform uses less memory in most implementations in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: FTransform enforces valid decomposition (no shear, uniform scale or separate axes). FMatrix can accumulate numerical error and non-rigid deformations.
questions.explanation: FTransform stores (Rotation, Translation, Scale) separately. Re-normalize quaternion to prevent drift. FMatrix is general 4×4 — error accumulates in all 16 entries, can introduce skew/shear.

questions.question: What does FMath::Lerp do?
options: Rotates for typical use cases
Linear interpolation: A + (B-A)·Alpha. Alpha=0 → A; Alpha=1 → B.
Clamps as defined in the specification with the default setupxxxx
Normalizes across all supported platforms for common use casesxx
questions.correctAnswer: Linear interpolation: A + (B-A)·Alpha. Alpha=0 → A; Alpha=1 → B.
questions.explanation: Lerp(A, B, Alpha) blends between A and B. Unclamped: Alpha outside [0,1] extrapolates. Used everywhere: animation blending, camera smoothing, color transitions.

questions.question: What does FMath::Clamp do?
options: Interpolates across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Constrains a value between Min and Max: if X < Min → Min; if X > Max → Max; else → X
Averages within the current scope with additional processing under normal operationxxxxx
Finds the maximum within the current scope
questions.correctAnswer: Constrains a value between Min and Max: if X < Min → Min; if X > Max → Max; else → X
questions.explanation: Clamp(X, Min, Max) ensures X ∈ [Min, Max]. Used for player input bounds, camera pitch limits, health bar percentages.

questions.question: What does FMath::Sin/Cos use — radians or degrees?
options: Degrees in most implementations
Radians — all UE math uses radians internally
Both — configurable by default behavior with t
Neither within the current scope with regular us
questions.correctAnswer: Radians — all UE math uses radians internally
questions.explanation: UE's FMath trig functions take radians. FMath::DegreesToRadians() and RadiansToDegrees() convert. FRotator stores degrees for editor display only.

questions.question: What does FMath::FInterpConstantTo do?
options: Lerp via the core framework
Constant-speed movement toward target — moves by fixed amount per second, stops at target
Easing under normal conditions with regular usage patternsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Rotation using standard engine approaches under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Constant-speed movement toward target — moves by fixed amount per second, stops at target
questions.explanation: FInterpConstantTo(Current, Target, DeltaTime, Speed) moves toward target at constant rate. Unlike FInterpTo (exponential), this moves linearly and stops exactly at target.

questions.question: What does FMath::FindDeltaAngle return?
options: An absolute angle for typical use cases in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
The shortest signed angular distance between two angles in degrees — e.g., -5° vs 355° returns -10°
A vector in most implementations
A quaternion through the built-in API with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: The shortest signed angular distance between two angles in degrees — e.g., -5° vs 355° returns -10°
questions.explanation: FindDeltaAngle(-5, 355) = -10 (degrees). Handles wrap-around at ±180°. Used for: smoothly rotating toward target, camera look-at interpolation.

questions.question: What is the closest point on a line segment to a point?
options: The midpoint via the core framework with additional processing
Project the point onto the line, clamp the parameter t to [0,1]
The endpoint under normal conditions in standard configurationsx
The origin for typical use cases
questions.correctAnswer: Project the point onto the line, clamp the parameter t to [0,1]
questions.explanation: t = dot(P-A, B-A) / dot(B-A, B-A). Clamp t to [0,1]. Closest point = A + t·(B-A). For t<0 → A; for t>1 → B; otherwise the projection.

questions.question: What geometric operation does the dot product enable for collision?
options: Area calculation across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Projecting one vector onto another — used for closest point, separation axis tests, and distance to plane
Rotation by default behavior
Volume calculation under normal conditions within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Projecting one vector onto another — used for closest point, separation axis tests, and distance to plane
questions.explanation: Dot product measures how parallel two vectors are. a·b = |a||b|cos(θ). Essential for: point-to-line projection, plane distance, SAT overlap testing.

questions.question: What is the difference between a ray cast and a sweep in UE?
options: Same by default behavior with additional processing
Ray cast: infinitely thin line query. Sweep: moves a shape (sphere, box, capsule) along a path, detecting overlaps. Sweep is more expensive but catches thin geometry.
Sweep is faster by default behavior
Ray cast is deprecated under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Ray cast: infinitely thin line query. Sweep: moves a shape (sphere, box, capsule) along a path, detecting overlaps. Sweep is more expensive but catches thin geometry.
questions.explanation: LineTraceSingle = ray cast (thin). SweepSingleByChannel = sweep with shape. Sweep prevents tunneling through small gaps. Use ray for bullets/projectiles; use sweep for character movement.

questions.question: What does FMath::LineBoxIntersection compute?
options: Box volume through the built-in API under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Whether a line segment intersects an axis-aligned box — slab method: compute t_entry/t_exit per axis, check overlap
Box surface area using standard engine approaches in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Box center under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: Whether a line segment intersects an axis-aligned box — slab method: compute t_entry/t_exit per axis, check overlap
questions.explanation: Slab method: for each axis, compute t_near=(min-O)/D, t_far=(max-O)/D. t_enter = max(t_nears), t_exit = min(t_fars). Hit if t_enter ≤ t_exit. Handles parallel rays (D=0).

questions.question: What is a convex hull used for in collision detection?
options: Rendering via the core framework with additional processing in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Approximating complex shapes with a tight-fitting convex polyhedron — GJK/EPA can collide any convex shape efficiently
Texture mapping as defined in the specification for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Animation across all supported platforms
questions.correctAnswer: Approximating complex shapes with a tight-fitting convex polyhedron — GJK/EPA can collide any convex shape efficiently
questions.explanation: Convex hull = shrink-wrap around mesh vertices. Simplifies collision geometry while maintaining tight bounds. GJK algorithm handles any convex shape. Generated offline or at runtime.

questions.question: What is FRichCurve in UE?
options: A single keyframe under normal conditions in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A cubic Bezier curve with key-value pairs and configurable tangents — used for animation, timeline, and material curves
A random number generator across all supported platforms with the default setupxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A color curve in most implementations
questions.correctAnswer: A cubic Bezier curve with key-value pairs and configurable tangents — used for animation, timeline, and material curves
questions.explanation: FRichCurve stores keyframes with Arrive/Leave tangents. Evaluates via cubic Hermite interpolation between keys. Used in UCurveFloat, TimelineComponent, material instances.

questions.question: What does FMath::RandRange do?
options: Returns 0 or 1
Returns a random float in [Min, Max]
Generates a random vector in standard confi
Generates a random integer only
questions.correctAnswer: Returns a random float in [Min, Max]
questions.explanation: FMath::RandRange(Min, Max) uses FRandomStream internally. RandRange with ints → integer in [Min, Max]. Seed with FRandomStream for deterministic randomness.

questions.question: What is an FRuntimeFloatCurve?
options: A compile-time curve as defined in the specification under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
An optimized curve structure for runtime evaluation — stores pre-baked lookup from FRichCurve for fast access without keyframe iteration
A Blueprint curve in most implementations
A material curve via the core framework with additional processing in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: An optimized curve structure for runtime evaluation — stores pre-baked lookup from FRichCurve for fast access without keyframe iteration
questions.explanation: FRichCurve = editor-friendly with keyframes and tangents. FRuntimeFloatCurve = bakes to uniform samples for O(1) runtime evaluation. Used for gameplay curves that need fast evaluation.

questions.question: What does FRandomStream provide that FMath::Rand doesn't?
options: Nothing in most implementations
Deterministic, seedable random sequence — same seed always produces the same sequence. Essential for procedural generation that needs to be reproducible.
Faster random in most implementations
Crypto-safe random using standard engine approaches
questions.correctAnswer: Deterministic, seedable random sequence — same seed always produces the same sequence. Essential for procedural generation that needs to be reproducible.
questions.explanation: FRandomStream(Seed) creates a reproducible RNG. FMath::Rand() uses a global RNG — non-deterministic between runs. Use FRandomStream for procedural content that must be consistent across sessions.

questions.question: What is FMath::SRand() for?
options: Random floats only as defined in the specification across common platformsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Seeds the global RNG — sets the seed for subsequent FMath::Rand() calls. Useful for testing with reproducible randomness.
Nothing by default behavior
String randomization within the current scope within the standard frameworkxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Seeds the global RNG — sets the seed for subsequent FMath::Rand() calls. Useful for testing with reproducible randomness.
questions.explanation: SRand(Seed) seeds UE's global RNG. All subsequent FMath::Rand() calls produce deterministic sequence for that seed. Use for: test reproducibility, network-synced random events.

questions.question: What does a condition variable solve?
options: Mutual exclusion using standard engine approaches
Blocking a thread until a condition becomes true
Memory ordering as defined in the specification
Thread creation as defined in the specification with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: Blocking a thread until a condition becomes true
questions.explanation: A CV allows threads to wait (sleep) until notified that a condition holds. cv.wait() atomically releases the mutex and blocks. cv.notify_one() wakes one waiter.

questions.question: Why must a condition variable always be used with a mutex?
options: Performance through the built-in API
To protect the shared condition state from concurrent modification
It's required by the C++ standard without reason
Historical legacy within the current scope with additional processing
questions.correctAnswer: To protect the shared condition state from concurrent modification
questions.explanation: The condition (e.g., queue not empty) is shared state. The mutex prevents races between checking the condition and waiting on the CV — without it, a wakeup could be missed.

questions.question: What is the difference between notify_one and notify_all?
options: No difference via the core framework with additional processing
notify_one wakes one waiting thread; notify_all wakes all
notify_all is faster under normal conditions
notify_one is deprecated under normal conditions
questions.correctAnswer: notify_one wakes one waiting thread; notify_all wakes all
questions.explanation: notify_one: unblocks one arbitrary waiter (good for single-consumer). notify_all: unblocks all waiters (needed when different threads wait for different conditions on the same CV).

questions.question: What does spurious wakeup mean?
options: A bug in the kernel for typical use cases
wait() can return even without a corresponding notify() — always check the condition in a while loop
A performance optimization by default behavior in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
A type of deadlock across all supported platforms in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: wait() can return even without a corresponding notify() — always check the condition in a while loop
questions.explanation: The OS may wake a thread waiting on a CV for no reason. Always wrap wait() in 'while(!condition) cv.wait(lock)' — never use 'if'.

questions.question: What does cv.wait(lock, predicate) do?
options: Just waits under normal conditions
Atomically releases the mutex and sleeps until the predicate is true — equivalent to while(!pred()) cv.wait(lock)
Only checks predicate within the current scope in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Does nothing special in most implementations under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Atomically releases the mutex and sleeps until the predicate is true — equivalent to while(!pred()) cv.wait(lock)
questions.explanation: The two-argument wait is the recommended form. It handles spurious wakeups internally by re-checking the predicate on each wakeup.

questions.question: How does Cast<T> achieve O(1) without RTTI?
options: It doesn't by default behavior
UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags<T>. Cast compares flags as integer AND — no vtable walk.
dynamic_cast in most implementations in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
reinterpret_cast by default behavior under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: UHT generates StaticClass() with TCastFlags<T>. Cast compares flags as integer AND — no vtable walk.
questions.explanation: Each UClass has a bitmask of cast flags (EClassCastFlags). Cast<T> checks GetClass()->HasAnyCastFlag(TCastFlags<T>::value). Single integer operation.

questions.question: What is CastChecked?
options: A slower Cast through the built-in API
Asserts/crashes if the cast fails — use when the type MUST be correct
Returns nullptr within the current scope in most practical scenariosxxxxxxxx
A deprecated function using standard engine approaches within the standar
questions.correctAnswer: Asserts/crashes if the cast fails — use when the type MUST be correct
questions.explanation: CastChecked<T>(Obj) = 'I know Obj is T'. Crashes on failure (better than silent nullptr bug). Use in controlled paths where failure = logic error.

questions.question: What does DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS do?
options: Nothing visible as defined in the specification
Generates a compile-time bitmask for fast Cast<T> checks — one bit per class in the hierarchy
Marks a class as abstract for typical use cases under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Creates a Blueprint class using standard engine approaches with additional processing in most practi
questions.correctAnswer: Generates a compile-time bitmask for fast Cast<T> checks — one bit per class in the hierarchy
questions.explanation: DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS(T) creates TCastFlags_V<T> for O(1) cast checking. Used by Cast<T>(). Without it, Cast falls back to IsA<T>() which is slower.

questions.question: What does DECLARE_CASTCLASS_FLAGS do?
options: Nothing for typical use cases
Generates a compile-time bitmask (TCastFlags_V<T>) for O(1) type checking per class
Creates a Blueprint class via the core framework in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxx
Marks a class as abstract by default behavior in standard configurationsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Generates a compile-time bitmask (TCastFlags_V<T>) for O(1) type checking per class
questions.explanation: Each UCLASS gets a unique bit in the cast flags mask. Cast<T> checks if the object's class has that bit set — one integer AND operation.

questions.question: What happens if you use Cast<T> on an object pending kill?
options: Returns nullptr by default behavior with additional processing under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
Still succeeds — pending kill objects retain class flags but IsValid() returns false. Always check IsValid after Cast.
Crashes across all supported platforms
Returns the object across all supported platforms under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Still succeeds — pending kill objects retain class flags but IsValid() returns false. Always check IsValid after Cast.
questions.explanation: Pending kill objects pass IsA()/Cast() but are about to be destroyed. Use IsValid(Obj) which checks both nullptr and pending kill state.

questions.question: How does Cast<T> compare to dynamic_cast in terms of performance?
options: Same speed for typical use cases
Cast<T> is ~10x faster — one integer AND vs dynamic_cast's vtable walk + type_info comparison
Cast<T> is slower across all supported platforms with typical engine settingsxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
dynamic_cast doesn't exist in UE under normal conditions under normal operationxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
questions.correctAnswer: Cast<T> is ~10x faster — one integer AND vs dynamic_cast's vtable walk + type_info comparison
questions.explanation: dynamic_cast walks the class hierarchy and compares type_info objects. UE's Cast<T> uses static class flags generated at compile time — a single integer bitmask check.

questions.question: When should you use Cast<T> vs IsA<T>?
options: No difference using standard engine approaches
Cast<T>: do something WITH the result (calls methods). IsA<T>: just checking (bool, no dereference).
Cast is always better by default behavior with additional processing for common use casesxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxxx
