questions.explanation: `std::lock(m1, m2, ...)` uses a deadlock-free algorithm: try to lock all mutexes with `try_lock`. If any fails, unlock all acquired ones, and retry. This breaks the 'hold and wait' condition. `std::scoped_lock` is the RAII wrapper that calls `std::lock`.

questions.question: What execution units do SMT (Hyper-Threading) logical threads share on a physical core?
options: Only the L1 data cache
Execution units (ALUs, FPUs) and caches — only the architectural state is duplicated
Nothing is shared — each logical core is fully independent
Only the branch predictor
questions.correctAnswer: Execution units (ALUs, FPUs) and caches — only the architectural state is duplicated
questions.explanation: SMT duplicates architectural state (register files, APIC IDs) but shares execution units and caches between two logical threads on the same physical core. This yields only 15-30% throughput improvement for typical workloads, not 100%, because the threads compete for shared compute resources.

questions.question: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
options: A value changes from A to B to A between read and CAS — the CAS succeeds but the structure may be corrupted
The atomic operation always returns the same value
Two threads get stuck in an infinite CAS loop
The memory ordering is too weak
questions.correctAnswer: A value changes from A to B to A between read and CAS — the CAS succeeds but the structure may be corrupted
questions.explanation: Thread 1 reads pointer A, is preempted. Thread 2 frees A, allocates a new object (also at address A), changes the structure. Thread 1 resumes, CAS succeeds (still A!), but the pointer's meaning has changed — leading to corruption. Solutions: tagged pointers (version counter in upper bits) or hazard pointers.

questions.question: In the game engine concurrency model, what is the rule about shared mutable state?
options: All state should be shared for maximum parallelism
Shared mutable state is the enemy — minimize it; use lock-free queues to communicate between threads
Shared state should always be protected by a single global lock
Mutable state should never be shared — all data must be immutable
questions.correctAnswer: Shared mutable state is the enemy — minimize it; use lock-free queues to communicate between threads
questions.explanation: The game thread updates game state; worker threads process data independently; lock-free queues (SPSC/MPMC) communicate results. Minimizing shared mutable state reduces lock contention, false sharing, and the surface area for data races. This is the core philosophy behind job-system architectures.

questions.question: Using Amdahl's Law, explain why adding more cores eventually provides diminishing returns. Calculate the maximum speedup for a game loop that is 80% parallelizable.
questions.explanation: With S = 0.20 (20% serial): maximum speedup = 1/0.20 = 5×, regardless of core count. With 4 cores: speedup = 1/(0.20 + 0.80/4) = 1/0.40 = 2.5×. With 16 cores: 1/(0.20 + 0.80/16) = 1/0.25 = 4×. Adding cores from 4→16 only gains 1.5× more speedup because the 20% serial portion dominates. This is why engines focus on reducing the serial fraction rather than just adding threads.

questions.question: Describe the false sharing problem from the Concurrency Summary. Why does padding with `alignas(64)` fix it, and what's the performance difference?
questions.explanation: Two threads updating adjacent variables on the same 64-byte cache line cause constant MESI invalidations — each write invalidates the other core's cache line. Performance drops 50-200 cycles per access instead of 1 cycle. `alignas(64)` places each variable on its own cache line, so writes don't interfere. The performance difference can be 50-100× between the false-sharing case and the properly-padded case.

questions.question: According to the Hardware Stack table, which Note covers 'the defining performance constraint'?
options: 01 — Transistors & Logic Gates
05 — Memory Hierarchy
04 — CPU Architecture
07 — VM Full Picture
questions.correctAnswer: 05 — Memory Hierarchy
questions.explanation: Note 05 — Memory Hierarchy is described as covering caches, locality, and 'the memory wall — the defining performance constraint.' The memory wall refers to the 300-cycle DRAM access gap that dominates modern CPU performance.

questions.question: Which latency hides the fact that the CPU can execute 400+ instructions during one DRAM access?
options: The OS scheduler switching threads during the wait
Caches, prefetchers, TLBs, branch predictors — all are latency-hiding mechanisms
Hyper-Threading executing another thread's instructions
The CPU enters a low-power state until the data arrives
questions.correctAnswer: Caches, prefetchers, TLBs, branch predictors — all are latency-hiding mechanisms
questions.explanation: Every level of the memory hierarchy (L1/L2/L3 caches, hardware prefetchers, TLBs, branch predictors) exists to hide the next level's latency. The CPU can execute 400+ instructions while waiting for one DRAM access — all these mechanisms work to keep the pipeline fed with useful work during that wait.

questions.question: According to the Hardware Stack, what is the key insight at the 'Physics' layer?
options: Voltage determines switching speed
NAND gates are universal — every computation ultimately reduces to them
Transistors must be cooled to function
Quantum effects dominate at 5nm
questions.correctAnswer: NAND gates are universal — every computation ultimately reduces to them
questions.explanation: Note 01 — Transistors & Logic Gates: the key insight is that NAND gates are functionally complete — any boolean function can be built from NAND gates alone. Every computation in your CPU, from addition to branch prediction, decomposes into NAND gate networks at the transistor level.

questions.question: How many CPU cycles does an HDD seek cost at 3 GHz?
options: ~150,000 cycles
~300 cycles
questions.explanation: HDD seek: ~10 ms. At 3 GHz (0.33 ns/cycle): 10,000,000 ns ÷ 0.33 ns = ~30,000,000 cycles. Compare: L1 hit = 3 cycles, DRAM access = 300 cycles. The 100,000× gap from L1 to HDD is why storage I/O is the ultimate performance killer.

questions.question: What is the second 'Big Idea' from the Hardware Summary?
options: Faster hardware is always better
Everything is an abstraction over physics — understanding the layer below tells you what can go wrong
Moore's Law guarantees performance improvements
The OS is the most important layer
questions.correctAnswer: Everything is an abstraction over physics — understanding the layer below tells you what can go wrong
questions.explanation: The summary lists five examples: virtual memory abstracts DRAM + disk, filesystems abstract NAND pages, TCP abstracts IP packets, threads abstract CPU cores, GPU draw calls abstract SIMT threads. Understanding the layer below reveals failure modes and measurement points.

questions.question: Which Hardware note covers Spectre, Meltdown, and Rowhammer vulnerabilities?
options: 13 — Interrupts & Exceptions
16 — Hardware Security
14 — Virtualization & Containers
04 — CPU Architecture
questions.correctAnswer: 16 — Hardware Security
questions.explanation: Note 16 — Hardware Security covers speculative execution attacks (Spectre, Meltdown) and DRAM disturbance attacks (Rowhammer). The key insight: 'the hardware itself is the attack surface' — these vulnerabilities exploit the microarchitecture, not software bugs.

questions.question: According to the summary, what does the OS kernel (ring 0) virtualize?
options: Only the filesystem
The CPU (threads), memory (page tables + swap), and I/O (drivers + interrupts)
Only network access
The compiler and linker
questions.correctAnswer: The CPU (threads), memory (page tables + swap), and I/O (drivers + interrupts)
questions.explanation: The kernel virtualizes: CPU → threads (preemptive scheduling), Memory → page tables + swap (virtual address spaces), I/O → drivers + interrupts (uniform file/device interface). User-space (ring 3) builds on these abstractions via syscalls.

questions.question: What does the 'Two Big Ideas' section identify as the purpose of every level of the memory hierarchy?
options: To increase total storage capacity
To hide the next level's latency
To reduce manufacturing costs
To simplify the programming model
questions.correctAnswer: To hide the next level's latency
questions.explanation: The summary explicitly states: 'Every level of the memory hierarchy exists to hide the next level's latency. Caches, prefetchers, TLBs, branch predictors — all of them are latency-hiding mechanisms.' Latency, not capacity or cost, is the driving design force.

questions.question: The Hardware Summary lists latency numbers from L1 cache (1 ns) to HDD seek (10 ms). Explain why this 10-million-to-1 ratio defines how game engines must design their data access patterns.
questions.explanation: The 10,000,000:1 ratio means accessing data from the wrong level of the hierarchy is catastrophic. Game engines respond with: data-oriented design (store hot data contiguously for cache line utilization), LOD systems (reduce data resolution at distance rather than loading all), texture streaming (load only visible mip levels), and pool allocators (keep game objects in slabs for TLB/cache locality). If a game loop randomly accesses HDD-backing storage, one 10 ms seek blows a 16.67 ms frame budget. Every performance optimization is ultimately about keeping data as high in the hierarchy as possible.

questions.question: The Hardware Stack spans from transistors to GPU architecture. Pick two non-adjacent layers and explain how a problem at one layer manifests at the other. Use a concrete example.
questions.explanation: Example: Transistors (Layer 1) → CPU Architecture (Layer 4). DRAM cells use capacitors that leak charge (transistor-level physics). This requires refresh cycles (tREFI ≈ 7.8 µs). During refresh, the DRAM bank is unavailable, adding latency variability to every memory access. The CPU's out-of-order engine (Layer 4) must handle this by keeping a deep instruction window to find independent work during DRAM stalls. Rowhammer (Layer 1 to Layer 16): repeated DRAM row activations cause bit flips in adjacent rows due to capacitive coupling — a physics-level effect exploitable as a security vulnerability (Layer 16).

questions.question: What are the Five Pillars of RAM & Virtual Memory as listed in the summary?
options: Speed, Capacity, Cost, Power, Reliability
Physics, Translation, Demand, Scale, Big Picture
Allocation, Deallocation, Fragmentation, Coalescing, Pooling
Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Write-back
questions.correctAnswer: Physics, Translation, Demand, Scale, Big Picture
questions.explanation: The five pillars: Physics (01 — DRAM Cells: 1T1C, refresh, row hammer), Translation (02 — Page Tables & TLB: 4-level paging, TLB caching), Demand (03 — Page Faults & mmap: lazy allocation, COW, swap), Scale (04 — Huge Pages & NUMA: 2MB/1GB pages, multi-socket), Big Picture (00 — Overview: the memory wall, traps).

questions.question: How many TLB entries are in the L1 data TLB, and what address range do they cover with standard 4 KB pages?
options: 32 entries covering 128 KB
64 entries covering 256 KB
128 entries covering 512 KB
1536 entries covering 6 MB
questions.correctAnswer: 64 entries covering 256 KB
questions.explanation: L1 D-TLB: 64 entries × 4 KB = 256 KB coverage. This is the 'pointer maze' trap: with 10,000 unique_ptr objects scattered across pages, you need 10,000 TLB entries but only have 64 — resulting in a 99.4% TLB miss rate. The L2 STLB (1536 entries) covers 6 MB.

questions.question: What is the 'First-Touch NUMA Trap'?
options: The first thread to access a page determines its NUMA node — if the allocating thread is on a different socket than the workers, all accesses are remote
The first memory access is always the slowest
NUMA requires touching each page before allocation
The OS allocates all memory on NUMA node 0 first
questions.correctAnswer: The first thread to access a page determines its NUMA node — if the allocating thread is on a different socket than the workers, all accesses are remote
questions.explanation: Linux's default memory policy is first-touch: the physical page is allocated from the NUMA node of the first thread that writes to it. If the main thread (Socket 0) allocates and initializes data, but worker threads (Socket 1) process it, all accesses are remote (150-250 ns vs 100 ns local). Fix: have Socket 1 workers touch the data first.

questions.question: When does swap thrashing begin according to the RAM & VM Summary?
options: When swap space exceeds RAM size
When the working set exceeds physical RAM and every frame triggers major page faults
When swap is on an HDD instead of SSD
When the system has less than 4 GB of RAM
questions.correctAnswer: When the working set exceeds physical RAM and every frame triggers major page faults
questions.explanation: Thrashing: working set > physical RAM → every frame triggers major page faults (reading pages from swap). The game doesn't just slow down — it effectively stops. `mlock` can pin critical buffers, but over-locking starves the rest of the system.

questions.question: What is the cost ratio between a minor page fault and a major page fault to HDD?
questions.explanation: Minor fault: ~1 µs (page already in RAM, just wire up PTE). Major fault to HDD: ~10 ms (seek + rotational latency + read). Ratio: 10,000 µs ÷ 1 µs = 10,000×. This is why `mlock` and pre-faulting strategies are critical for latency-sensitive applications.

questions.question: In the memory wall diagram, how many CPU cycles does a TLB miss plus page walk cost?
options: ~40-50 cycles
~300-350 cycles
~3,000 cycles
questions.explanation: A TLB miss triggers a hardware page walk through 4 levels of page tables (PML4→PDPT→PD→PT), requiring 4 dependent memory reads. Each read may hit in cache (fast, ~50 ns total) or miss to DRAM (slow, ~400 ns total). The page walk cost varies based on cache behavior.

questions.question: What does the summary identify as the 'Three Deadly Patterns' for memory performance?
options: Double-free, use-after-free, and memory leaks
The Pointer Maze, First-Touch NUMA Trap, and Swap Thrashing
Cache misses, TLB misses, and branch mispredictions
Stack overflow, heap fragmentation, and dangling pointers
questions.correctAnswer: The Pointer Maze, First-Touch NUMA Trap, and Swap Thrashing
questions.explanation: Three deadly patterns: (1) Pointer Maze — scattered allocations causing TLB thrashing; (2) First-Touch NUMA Trap — allocating on wrong socket causing remote access penalties; (3) Swap Thrashing — working set exceeding RAM causing constant major faults.

questions.question: How are DRAM cells physically organized?
options: As a stack of magnetic platters
As 1T1C cells (one transistor, one trench capacitor) in rows and columns
As flip-flops made of 6 transistors each
As floating-gate MOSFETs in NAND strings
questions.correctAnswer: As 1T1C cells (one transistor, one trench capacitor) in rows and columns
questions.explanation: DRAM uses 1T1C cells: one access transistor and one trench capacitor per bit. Reads are destructive (sense amplifier detects voltage, then writes back). Charge leaks through the transistor junction, requiring periodic refresh at tREFI ≈ 7.8 µs per row. SRAM (6T flip-flops) is faster but ~100× larger per bit.

questions.question: Explain the Pointer Maze pattern. Why does `std::vector<std::unique_ptr<Enemy>>` with 10,000 enemies cause TLB thrashing, while `std::vector<Enemy>` fixes it? Include TLB entry calculations.
questions.explanation: Pointer Maze: 10,000 `unique_ptr<Enemy>` where each Enemy is `new`'d individually. Each allocation may land in a different 4 KB page → 10,000 pages → need 10,000 TLB entries. L1 D-TLB has only 64 entries → 99.4% miss rate. Contiguous `vector<Enemy>`: 10,000 enemies × ~40 bytes each ≈ 400 KB → fits in ~98 pages. 98 TLB entries needed, L2 STLB (1536 entries) handles it comfortably. Even if some spill from L1 D-TLB, the L2 STLB covers the working set.

questions.question: The Memory Wall diagram shows DRAM access at ~350 cycles. Walk through what happens at the hardware level during those 350 cycles: from the CPU issuing a load to the data arriving in a register.
questions.explanation: 1. CPU issues load → MMU translates VA→PA (TLB lookup, hopefully hit). 2. Memory controller decodes PA → channel, rank, bank, row, column. 3. If different row open: PRECHARGE (close current row, ~15 ns) → ACTIVATE (open new row, sense amplifiers read entire row into row buffer, ~15 ns). 4. CAS (Column Address Strobe): select column from row buffer, ~15 ns. 5. Data burst transferred over memory bus (8 transfers for 64-byte cache line). 6. Data travels through memory controller → interconnect → L3 cache → L2 → L1 → CPU register. Total: ~100 ns = 350 cycles at 3.5 GHz. Most time is spent in analog DRAM operations (precharge, activation, sensing).

questions.question: What is the sequential-to-random I/O performance ratio for HDDs vs SSDs?
options: ~250:1 for HDDs, ~2:1 for SSDs
~10:1 for HDDs, ~5:1 for SSDs
~1000:1 for HDDs, ~10:1 for SSDs
~50:1 for HDDs, ~1:1 for SSDs
questions.correctAnswer: ~250:1 for HDDs, ~2:1 for SSDs
questions.explanation: HDDs: sequential ~250 MB/s vs random ~1.5 MB/s = ~250:1 ratio (dominated by seek + rotational latency). SSDs: sequential ~14 GB/s (Gen5) vs random ~6 GB/s (1.5M IOPS × 4 KB) = ~2:1 ratio. This ~2:1 ratio is why SSDs revolutionized computing — random access became practical.

questions.question: What is Trap 2 in the Storage Summary: 'Small Random Writes Kill SSDs'?
options: SSDs physically break from too many writes
Writing one 4 KB page can trigger reading a 256-page block, erasing it (2-10 ms), then reprogramming — write amplification up to 256×
SSDs slow down after 1000 writes
Random writes cause the SSD controller to crash
questions.correctAnswer: Writing one 4 KB page can trigger reading a 256-page block, erasing it (2-10 ms), then reprogramming — write amplification up to 256×
questions.explanation: NAND flash: pages (4-16 KB) are the read/write unit; blocks (256+ pages) are the erase unit. A small random write to a full block: read entire block, erase block (2-10 ms), reprogram block with old data + new page. This is why SSDs use the FTL, garbage collection, and over-provisioning to minimize write amplification.

questions.question: What does the page cache do when `write(fd, data, 4096)` is called?
options: Immediately writes the data to the physical storage device
Copies data into a page cache page and marks it dirty — actual disk I/O happens later via writeback or explicit fsync()
Compresses the data before storing
Encrypts the data before sending to the storage driver
questions.correctAnswer: Copies data into a page cache page and marks it dirty — actual disk I/O happens later via writeback or explicit fsync()
questions.explanation: `write()` copies user data to a page cache page (in RAM) and marks it dirty. The data is NOT on disk yet. The kernel's writeback threads (`flush_work`) eventually write dirty pages to disk, or `fsync()` forces immediate writeback. A power loss before writeback means the data is lost.

questions.question: According to the I/O gap table, what is the sequential read speed of a Gen5 NVMe SSD?
options: ~2 TB/s
~14 GB/s
~50 GB/s
questions.correctAnswer: ~14 GB/s
questions.explanation: The I/O Gap table lists: L1 Cache ~2 TB/s, RAM (DDR5) ~50 GB/s, NVMe SSD (Gen5) ~14 GB/s, SATA SSD ~550 MB/s, HDD ~250 MB/s. Gen5 NVMe (PCIe 5.0 ×4) achieves ~14 GB/s sequential — still ~3.5× slower than DDR5 RAM.

questions.question: What `posix_fadvise()` flag would prevent a 50 GB texture streaming scan from polluting the page cache?
options: POSIX_FADV_NORMAL
POSIX_FADV_SEQUENTIAL
POSIX_FADV_DONTNEED
POSIX_FADV_WILLNEED
questions.correctAnswer: POSIX_FADV_DONTNEED
questions.explanation: Exercise 4 asks about preventing page cache pollution. `POSIX_FADV_DONTNEED` advises the kernel that the data won't be accessed again soon, allowing the kernel to evict those pages from the page cache after the read. Without it, a 50 GB scan would evict all other useful cached data.

questions.question: Why is `mmap` not always faster than `read()` for file I/O?
options: mmap uses more CPU
Page faults from random access through mmap'd files still trigger I/O — at 10 ms per HDD seek, that's only 100 random pages/second
mmap bypasses the page cache
mmap requires kernel mode for every access
questions.correctAnswer: Page faults from random access through mmap'd files still trigger I/O — at 10 ms per HDD seek, that's only 100 random pages/second
questions.explanation: Trap 3: `mmap` avoids the `read()` syscall overhead, but page faults are still I/O. Random access through an `mmap`'d file on HDD triggers one major page fault per new page — at 10 ms each, that's 100 pages/second (400 KB/s). Sequential access lets the kernel's readahead hide latency by pre-loading upcoming pages.

questions.question: What do `dirty_ratio` and `dirty_background_ratio` control in Linux?
options: The percentage of CPU time spent on I/O
When the kernel forces writeback of dirty page cache pages — `dirty_ratio` triggers blocking writeback, `dirty_background_ratio` triggers background writeback
The ratio of reads to writes on a storage device
The balance between SSD and HDD usage
questions.correctAnswer: When the kernel forces writeback of dirty page cache pages — `dirty_ratio` triggers blocking writeback, `dirty_background_ratio` triggers background writeback
questions.explanation: `dirty_background_ratio`: when dirty pages exceed this % of RAM, background writeback begins (non-blocking). `dirty_ratio`: when dirty pages exceed this higher %, `write()` calls block until writeback reduces the dirty count. `dirty_expire_centisecs` controls how long dirty data can sit before forced writeback.

questions.question: What is the write-ahead logging approach recommended for a save-game system that must not lose more than 5 seconds of progress?
options: Write the entire save file every 5 seconds with fsync
Write changes to a journal first, fsync the journal, then periodically update the main file — the journal ensures crash recovery
Use a database instead of a file
Write to two separate files on different drives
questions.correctAnswer: Write changes to a journal first, fsync the journal, then periodically update the main file — the journal ensures crash recovery
questions.explanation: Exercise 5: a write-ahead log (journal) records changes before applying them to the main save file. `fsync` on the journal (small, fast) guarantees the changes are durable. If a crash occurs, replay the journal on next load. The main file can be updated less frequently (e.g., every checkpoint), reducing `fsync` overhead on the large 10 MB file.

questions.question: Explain the difference between `fflush()`, `fclose()`, and `fsync()`. If a game calls `fwrite()` + `fclose()` on a save file and the power fails, where is the data? What extra call ensures it survives?
questions.explanation: `fwrite()` → data in stdio userspace buffer. `fflush()` → moves data from stdio buffer to kernel page cache (still in RAM). `fclose()` → calls `fflush()` + closes the file descriptor, but does NOT `fsync()`. Data is in the page cache (RAM) — power loss means data is gone. Only `fsync()` forces the kernel to write dirty pages to the storage device and waits for the device to confirm the write. The pattern: `write()` + `fsync()` + rename of temp file is the correct durable save approach.

questions.question: A game streams a 500 MB audio file via `read()` with 16 GB RAM and `dirty_ratio = 20`. Calculate how much dirty page cache can accumulate before `write()` blocks, and how long the kernel spends flushing at 3 GB/s SSD write speed.
questions.explanation: `dirty_ratio = 20` means when 20% of RAM is dirty pages, `write()` blocks. 20% of 16 GB = 3.2 GB of dirty pages. The streaming is reading (audio playback), not writing, so `dirty_ratio` doesn't directly affect reads. But if the system is also writing (save, logs), once dirty pages reach 3.2 GB, all `write()` calls block until the kernel flushes. At 3 GB/s SSD write speed, flushing 3.2 GB takes ~1.07 seconds — during which the application is stalled on any `write()` call. This is why write-intensive applications must manage dirty ratios.

questions.question: What are the Three Phases of the source code to running process journey?
options: Write, Build, Deploy
Compile, Link, Run
Parse, Optimize, Execute
Preprocess, Assemble, Load
questions.correctAnswer: Compile, Link, Run
questions.explanation: The three phases: (1) Compile: C++ → IR → optimization passes → assembly → .o object file. (2) Link: symbol resolution, relocation, PLT/GOT for dynamic libraries. (3) Run: kernel ELF loader maps segments, sets up stack/heap, jumps to `_start` which eventually calls `main()`.

questions.question: What is the vDSO trick and why is `clock_gettime` so fast?
options: It caches the time in L1 cache
The kernel maps read-only code + a shared time data page into every process — the 'syscall' is just a memory read with no ring transition
It uses a dedicated hardware timer register
It runs at real-time priority
questions.correctAnswer: The kernel maps read-only code + a shared time data page into every process — the 'syscall' is just a memory read with no ring transition
questions.explanation: vDSO (`linux-vdso.so.1`) maps kernel-provided code into user space. For `clock_gettime`, the kernel updates a shared page (`vvar`) with time data using a seq-lock protocol. The 'syscall' is just a memory read (~20 ns) with no ring transition (~70 cycles saved).

questions.question: What does the linker's PLT/GOT mechanism enable for dynamic libraries?
options: Static linking of all symbols at compile time
Lazy binding — function addresses are resolved on first call via `_dl_runtime_resolve`, not at load time
Inlining of library functions into the executable
Preloading of all library code at startup
questions.correctAnswer: Lazy binding — function addresses are resolved on first call via `_dl_runtime_resolve`, not at load time
questions.explanation: PLT (Procedure Linkage Table) + GOT (Global Offset Table): on first call to a dynamic function, the PLT stub calls `_dl_runtime_resolve` which finds the symbol, patches the GOT entry, and jumps to the function. Subsequent calls go directly through the patched GOT — zero resolution overhead.

questions.question: What is the kernel's ELF loader (`load_elf_binary`) responsible for at process startup?
options: Compiling the source code
Reading program headers, mapping segments into memory (text=RX, data=RW), setting up stack/heap, and jumping to _start
Optimizing the machine code
Resolving dynamic library symbols
questions.correctAnswer: Reading program headers, mapping segments into memory (text=RX, data=RW), setting up stack/heap, and jumping to _start
questions.explanation: The ELF loader in `fs/binfmt_elf.c`: maps PT_LOAD segments via `elf_map()`, sets up the initial stack with argc/argv/envp/auxv, creates the initial brk heap, and transfers control to `e_entry` (typically `_start` in crt1.o). The dynamic linker resolves shared library symbols.

questions.question: How many CPU cycles does the x86-64 `syscall` instruction approximately cost?
options: ~1 cycle
~20 cycles
~70 cycles
~500 cycles
questions.correctAnswer: ~70 cycles
questions.explanation: The `syscall` instruction (via LSTAR MSR) costs ~70 cycles — it switches to ring 0, saves RIP/RFLAGS, and jumps to the kernel's syscall handler. The legacy `int 0x80` is much slower. The total syscall overhead (instruction + handler entry/exit) is ~1 µs for a trivial syscall like `getpid()`.

questions.question: In the compiler pipeline, what stage converts the AST to LLVM IR / GIMPLE?
options: Preprocessor
Lexer
Semantic analysis / IR generation
Assembler
questions.correctAnswer: Semantic analysis / IR generation
questions.explanation: After the parser builds the AST, semantic analysis performs type checking and overload resolution, then IR generation lowers the AST to an intermediate representation (LLVM IR for Clang, GIMPLE for GCC). Optimization passes then transform the IR before code generation produces target assembly.

questions.question: What is the purpose of relocation entries (e.g., R_X86_64_PC32) in object files?
options: To move the .text section to a different address
To mark addresses that can't be known until link/load time — the linker patches them with the final address
To encrypt symbol addresses
To compress the object file
questions.correctAnswer: To mark addresses that can't be known until link/load time — the linker patches them with the final address
questions.explanation: Relocation entries tell the linker: 'at this offset in the code, there's a reference to symbol X — patch it with X's final address plus this addend.' R_X86_64_PC32 is PC-relative: the patched value = S + A - P (symbol + addend - PC). This enables position-independent code.

questions.question: What does `_start` do before calling `main()`?
options: Initializes global variables to zero
Calls `__libc_start_main` which initializes libc, processes argv/envp, calls global constructors (.init_array), then calls main()
Sets up the stack canary
Performs Just-In-Time compilation of hot functions
questions.correctAnswer: Calls `__libc_start_main` which initializes libc, processes argv/envp, calls global constructors (.init_array), then calls main()
questions.explanation: `_start` (in crt1.o) is the real entry point. It sets up argc/argv on the stack and calls `__libc_start_main`, which: initializes libc internals, sets up the environment, runs global constructors (functions in `.init_array` section), and finally calls `main(argc, argv, envp)`. After `main` returns, it calls `exit()`.

questions.question: Trace the full compilation of `int add(int a, int b) { return a + b; }` from C++ source to the `call` instruction that invokes it. Name every stage and the key transformation at each.
questions.explanation: 1. Preprocessor: expands #include, #define (trivial for this function). 2. Lexer: text → tokens (int, add, (, int, a, ...). 3. Parser: tokens → AST (FunctionDecl with body ReturnStmt containing BinaryOp +). 4. Semantic analysis: type checking, resolves `a` and `b` to int parameters. 5. IR generation (LLVM IR): `define i32 @add(i32 %a, i32 %b) { %sum = add i32 %a, %b; ret i32 %sum }`. 6. Optimization: at -O2, the function is trivial enough to inline at call sites. 7. Code generation (x86-64): `leal (%rdi,%rsi), %eax; ret`. 8. Assembler: assembly → bytes in .text section of .o file. 9. Linker: resolves `add` symbol, produces final executable.

questions.question: Write `getpid()` in a tight loop vs `clock_gettime()` — the former does a real syscall (~70 cycles + ~1 µs), the latter uses vDSO (~20 ns). Explain why vDSO is faster at the hardware level (ring transitions, privilege checks, TLB behavior).
questions.explanation: `syscall` instruction: switches from ring 3 to ring 0 → saves RIP/RFLAGS to RCX/R11 → loads new RIP from LSTAR MSR → kernel saves all registers via SAVE_REGS → executes syscall handler → restores registers → `sysretq` back to ring 3. Each ring transition flushes the TLB (partially, mitigated by PCID) and changes privilege level. vDSO: runs in ring 3 (user mode). `clock_gettime` reads a value from the `vvar` page (mapped read-only) protected by a seq-lock: read sequence counter → read time data → re-read sequence counter (if changed, retry). No ring transition, no privilege check, no TLB flush — just a few memory reads.

questions.question: What is the approximate cost of a virtual function call through a vtable vs a non-virtual call?
options: Same cost — both are direct calls
Virtual call costs ~15-20 extra cycles: load vtable pointer → load function pointer → indirect call → possible branch mispredict
Virtual calls are 10× slower than non-virtual
Virtual calls have no extra cost on modern CPUs
questions.correctAnswer: Virtual call costs ~15-20 extra cycles: load vtable pointer → load function pointer → indirect call → possible branch mispredict
questions.explanation: A non-virtual call: `call known_address`. A virtual call: `mov rax, [rdi]` (load vtable pointer from object) → `call [rax + offset]` (indirect call through vtable). Two dependent loads before the branch target is known. If the branch predictor mispredicts the target (common with polymorphic calls), add ~15-20 cycles pipeline flush penalty.

questions.question: On System V AMD64, which registers hold the first four integer arguments?
options: rax, rbx, rcx, rdx
rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx
rcx, rdx, r8, r9
questions.correctAnswer: rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx
questions.explanation: System V AMD64 (Linux/macOS): first 6 integer args in rdi, rsi, rdx, rcx, r8, r9. Microsoft x64: first 4 in rcx, rdx, r8, r9. This difference is why Linux and Windows binaries are not ABI-compatible — the same function call compiles to different register assignments.

questions.question: What is a stack canary and how does it detect buffer overflows?
options: A compiler flag that warns about large stack frames
A random value placed between local variables and the saved return address — checked before `ret`; if corrupted, the overflow is detected
A hardware register that tracks stack bounds
A kernel feature that monitors stack growth
questions.correctAnswer: A random value placed between local variables and the saved return address — checked before `ret`; if corrupted, the overflow is detected
questions.explanation: Stack canary (`-fstack-protector`): a random value is placed between local buffers and the saved return address. Before the function returns, the canary is checked. If a buffer overflow wrote past the buffer, the canary is corrupted → the program aborts instead of returning to attacker-controlled address. The canary value is randomized per process (from /dev/urandom or AT_RANDOM).

questions.question: What does Intel CET (Control-flow Enforcement Technology) protect against?
options: Cache timing attacks
ROP/JOP attacks — a hardware shadow stack ensures `ret` pops the same address that the corresponding `call` pushed
SQL injection
Integer overflow bugs
questions.correctAnswer: ROP/JOP attacks — a hardware shadow stack ensures `ret` pops the same address that the corresponding `call` pushed
questions.explanation: CET adds a hardware shadow stack: every `call` pushes the return address to both the regular stack AND the shadow stack. `ret` pops from both and compares them — if they differ, a #CP (Control Protection) exception is raised. This prevents Return-Oriented Programming (ROP) attacks that overwrite return addresses.

questions.question: What is the calling convention mismatch trap described in the summary?
options: Compiling with the wrong optimization level
If a Windows DLL expects `stdcall` but you declare it as `cdecl`, the stack pointer is wrong after the call returns — causing corruption or crash
Using too many function arguments
Calling a function from the wrong thread
questions.correctAnswer: If a Windows DLL expects `stdcall` but you declare it as `cdecl`, the stack pointer is wrong after the call returns — causing corruption or crash
questions.explanation: `stdcall`: callee cleans the stack (`ret N`). `cdecl`: caller cleans the stack (`add rsp, N`). If you mismatch: both the caller AND callee try to clean (stack underflow) or NEITHER cleans (stack overflow). Either way, RSP is wrong after the call — the next `ret` pops garbage.

questions.question: What does ASLR (Address Space Layout Randomization) randomize?
options: Only the stack address
Code, data, stack, heap, and library base addresses — attackers can't hardcode addresses
The instruction execution order
The register allocation
questions.correctAnswer: Code, data, stack, heap, and library base addresses — attackers can't hardcode addresses
questions.explanation: ASLR randomizes the base addresses of the executable, libraries, stack, and heap at each process launch. This means even if an attacker discovers a vulnerability, they can't hardcode the address of their payload or a useful gadget — they must first leak an address via an information disclosure.

questions.question: What is the red zone in System V AMD64 ABI?
options: A security boundary in the kernel
128 bytes below RSP that leaf functions can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers won't touch it
A memory region reserved for debugging
The area between the stack and heap
questions.correctAnswer: 128 bytes below RSP that leaf functions can use without adjusting RSP — signal handlers won't touch it
questions.explanation: System V's red zone: 128 bytes below the current stack pointer reserved for the current function. Leaf functions (functions that don't call other functions) can use this space without `sub rsp, N` — saving a few instructions. Signal handlers are required to skip the red zone when delivering signals. Microsoft x64 does NOT have a red zone.

questions.question: What does `-fcf-protection` enable in GCC/Clang?
options: Stack canaries for all functions
Intel CET (shadow stack + indirect branch tracking)
Address sanitizer (ASan)
Control flow graph visualization
questions.correctAnswer: Intel CET (shadow stack + indirect branch tracking)
questions.explanation: `-fcf-protection=full` enables both CET features: `-fcf-protection=branch` (ENDBRANCH instructions for indirect branch tracking) and `-fcf-protection=return` (shadow stack for return address integrity). This requires a CPU with CET support (Intel Tiger Lake+).

questions.question: Draw the stack frame layout for `int foo(int a, int b, int c) { int d = a + b; return d + c; }` on System V AMD64 at -O0. Label: return address, saved RBP, local `d`, and where parameters `a`, `b`, `c` are. Include offsets from RBP.
questions.explanation: Stack layout (high to low address): [c] at RBP+16 (3rd arg, passed in RDX, spilled to stack at -O0), [b] at RBP+12 (from RSI), [a] at RBP+8 (from RDI), [return address] at RBP+8 (caller's, before push rbp — actually at RBP+8 after prologue), [saved RBP] at RBP+0 (old frame pointer), [d] at RBP-4 (local int). Note: at -O0, register args are spilled to the stack for debugging. At -O2, `d` is optimized out, args stay in registers, and the function may be inlined entirely.

questions.question: Compare the Microsoft x64 and System V AMD64 calling conventions for a function with 8 integer parameters. Where do parameters 7 and 8 go in each convention, and why does this prevent cross-OS binary compatibility?
questions.explanation: System V: params 1-6 in RDI, RSI, RDX, RCX, R8, R9. Params 7-8 go on the stack (right-to-left, caller cleanup). Red zone: 128 bytes below RSP usable by leaf functions. Microsoft x64: params 1-4 in RCX, RDX, R8, R9. Params 5-8 (and 7-8) go on the stack. Shadow space: 32 bytes reserved below return address. The different register assignments mean the same C++ function compiles to different assembly. A .so compiled for Linux (System V) expects args in RDI/RSI; a .dll compiled for Windows (MS x64) passes them in RCX/RDX — they're binary-incompatible even on the same x86-64 hardware.

questions.question: What does 'const int* p' mean?
options: Pointer to a constant integer — you cannot modify the integer through p
Constant pointer to an integer — you cannot change where p points
Both the pointer and the integer are constant
The pointer is constant but the integer is mutable
questions.correctAnswer: Pointer to a constant integer — you cannot modify the integer through p
questions.explanation: Read right-to-left: p is a pointer to an int that is const. The pointer itself can be reassigned to point elsewhere; only the pointed-to value is protected.

questions.question: Which of these correctly declares a const pointer to a mutable int?
options: const int* p
int* const p
const int* const p
int const* p
questions.correctAnswer: int* const p
questions.explanation: int* const p reads right-to-left as 'p is a const pointer to int.' The pointer cannot be reassigned, but the int it points to can be modified.

questions.question: What happens if you mark a member function as const?
options: The function cannot modify any member variables except those marked mutable
The function cannot be called on non-const objects
The function returns a const value
The function is evaluated at compile time
questions.correctAnswer: The function cannot modify any member variables except those marked mutable
questions.explanation: A const member function has an implicit 'const T* this' parameter. The compiler prevents modification of non-mutable members, enforced at compile time with zero runtime overhead.

questions.question: Why does 'const' on a pointer member NOT protect the pointed-to data?
options: const only applies to the pointer itself (int* const), not to what it points to
const is ignored for pointer members
Pointers are always mutable regardless of const
The compiler cannot track pointer ownership through const
questions.correctAnswer: const only applies to the pointer itself (int* const), not to what it points to
questions.explanation: const is shallow — it protects the top-level members only. A const method on a class with int* data prevents you from changing data (the pointer), but *data (the pointed-to value) is unprotected.

questions.question: When should you pass a function parameter by 'const T&' instead of by value?
options: When the object is large and you only need to read it — avoids an expensive copy
When you need to modify the original object
Always — const T& is always faster than pass-by-value
Only for primitive types like int and float
questions.correctAnswer: When the object is large and you only need to read it — avoids an expensive copy
questions.explanation: Pass by const reference avoids copying the object (no constructor/destructor overhead) while preventing accidental modification. For small types (int, pointers), pass by value is often faster because it avoids indirection.

questions.question: What is 'mutable' used for in a const member function?
options: To allow specific member variables to be modified even in const methods — used for caching, mutexes, and debug counters
To make the entire object modifiable despite const
To convert a const reference to a non-const reference
To bypass all const restrictions in the class
questions.correctAnswer: To allow specific member variables to be modified even in const methods — used for caching, mutexes, and debug counters
questions.explanation: mutable enables logical constness: the object's observable state doesn't change, but internal bookkeeping (cache invalidation, mutex locking, access counters) can be updated. Use sparingly — it's not for circumventing const correctness.

questions.question: What is the difference between top-level const and low-level const?
options: Top-level const applies to the variable itself and is ignored when copying; low-level const applies to what's pointed/referred to and must match
Top-level const means 'const at the top of the file'; low-level means 'const inside functions'
There is no difference — const always means the same thing
Top-level const is a C++17 feature; low-level const is pre-C++17
questions.correctAnswer: Top-level const applies to the variable itself and is ignored when copying; low-level const applies to what's pointed/referred to and must match
questions.explanation: int* const p has top-level const (p is const) — when copying p, the const is stripped. const int* p has low-level const (the int is const) — when assigning to another pointer, this const must be preserved. You can add low-level const but never remove it.

questions.question: What does 'const_cast' do and when should you use it?
options: Removes const from a reference/pointer — only for interop with legacy APIs that don't modify, never for modifying genuinely const objects
Adds const to any variable at compile time
Converts between const and constexpr
Safely removes const from any variable
questions.correctAnswer: Removes const from a reference/pointer — only for interop with legacy APIs that don't modify, never for modifying genuinely const objects
questions.explanation: const_cast is for edge cases: calling C APIs that take char* but don't modify, or passing const data to old libraries missing const annotations. Using const_cast to modify a variable declared const is undefined behavior.

questions.question: Which is true about 'const' and return types?
options: Returning 'const int' by value is pointless (caller gets a copy); returning 'const std::string&' is useful (caller can read without copying or modifying)
Always return const from functions to prevent modification
Never return const references — they dangle when the function exits
const return types prevent copy elision
questions.correctAnswer: Returning 'const int' by value is pointless (caller gets a copy); returning 'const std::string&' is useful (caller can read without copying or modifying)
questions.explanation: Top-level const on a return-by-value is meaningless since the caller receives a copy. Const references returned from member functions give read-only access to internal data without exposing it to modification.

questions.question: What's the difference between 'const' and 'constexpr'?
options: const means 'won't change after initialization' (runtime OK); constexpr means 'must be known at compile time'
They are synonyms — constexpr was added in C++11 as an alias
const is for variables; constexpr is only for functions
constexpr is weaker than const — constexpr values can change at runtime
questions.correctAnswer: const means 'won't change after initialization' (runtime OK); constexpr means 'must be known at compile time'
questions.explanation: const int x = GetRuntimeValue() is valid — the value is fixed after initialization but unknown at compile time. constexpr int y = 42 is evaluated entirely at compile time. All constexpr variables are implicitly const.
